questions.prompt:
Camels live in the hot desert. There is very little water in the desert. A camel can go many days without a drink.

Why is it helpful for a camel to go many days without water?
choices: Because the desert has very little water
Because the desert is very hot
Because camels do not like water
Because camels drink only at night
questions.correct: Because the desert has very little water
questions.hint: Put two facts together: what is hard to find in the desert, and what a camel can do without.
questions.whyHard: The benefit is never stated; the child must link 'little water' to why the camel's trait helps.

questions.prompt:
Bats sleep all day long. They wake up when the sky gets dark. After they wake up, bats fly around and catch bugs to eat.

When do bats look for food?
choices: In the morning
In the middle of the day
At night
Right after lunch
questions.correct: At night
questions.hint: Find when bats wake up, then find when they eat. Both clues point to the same time.
questions.whyHard: Two-step chain never says 'night': dark sky = night, and eating happens after waking.

questions.prompt:
A turtle carries a hard shell on its back. When a turtle is scared, it pulls its head and legs inside the shell. The shell keeps the turtle safe.

What will a turtle most likely do when a hungry fox comes close?
choices: Run away very fast
Hide inside its shell
Swim deep under the water
Climb up a tall tree
questions.correct: Hide inside its shell
questions.hint: A fox coming close is scary. What does the text say a turtle does when it feels scared?
questions.whyHard: Requires applying the stated rule (scared → hides) to a new situation the text never names.

questions.prompt:
Maple trees have green leaves in summer. In fall, the leaves turn red and orange. Then the leaves drop off. In winter, the tree has no leaves at all.

What color are the maple leaves in the season right before winter?
choices: Green
Red and orange
Brown and gray
There are no leaves
questions.correct: Red and orange
questions.hint: First figure out which season comes right before winter, then find that season's leaf color.
questions.whyHard: Multi-step: order the seasons to see 'fall,' then match fall to its color, avoiding the winter trap.

questions.prompt:
Firefighters wear thick coats to their job. Their coats are made so they do not burn easily. Firefighters get very close to fire when they work.

Why do firefighters wear coats that do not burn easily?
choices: To stay warm on cold days
To keep safe when they are close to fire
To look the same as each other
To run faster to the fire truck
questions.correct: To keep safe when they are close to fire
questions.hint: Think about where firefighters stand when they work, and what a coat that won't burn is good for there.
questions.whyHard: The tempting 'stay warm' matches what thick coats usually do; the child must use the text's fire clue instead.

questions.prompt: Read the text: "Bats sleep all day. They hang upside down in dark caves. At night, they fly out to catch bugs to eat." When do bats look for their food?
choices: In the morning
At night
While they sleep
Inside their nest
questions.correct: At night
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what bats do at night, and think about what "catch bugs to eat" means.
questions.whyHard: The text never says "food"; the child must connect "catch bugs to eat" with "food" and reject the tempting "all day" detail.

questions.prompt: Read the text: "A camel can go many days without water. It stores fat in its hump. When food is hard to find, the camel uses the fat to stay alive." Why is the hump helpful to a camel?
choices: It keeps the camel warm at night.
It helps the camel run very fast.
It holds fat the camel can use when there is no food.
It is full of the water the camel drinks.
questions.correct: It holds fat the camel can use when there is no food.
questions.hint: Read the last two sentences together. What is in the hump, and when does the camel use it?
questions.whyHard: Two sentences must be combined; the common belief that a hump holds water is a strong, plausible distractor the text quietly corrects.

questions.prompt: Read the text: "Bees live together in a hive. Worker bees fly out to find flowers. They bring food back so the baby bees can grow." What would most likely happen if the worker bees stopped flying out?
choices: The hive would get bigger.
The baby bees might not get food.
The flowers would close up.
More bees would fly out.
questions.correct: The baby bees might not get food.
questions.hint: The workers bring food back for a reason. What depends on that food?
questions.whyHard: Requires a cause-and-effect prediction beyond the stated facts, linking the workers' job to the baby bees' need.

questions.prompt: Read the text: "Some trees lose their leaves in the fall. The leaves turn red and gold, then drop to the ground. In spring, tiny green leaves grow back." Which season comes right BEFORE the tree grows new leaves?
choices: Winter
Fall
Spring
Summer
questions.correct: Winter
questions.hint: The text names two seasons. New leaves grow in spring, so think about the season that comes just before spring.
questions.whyHard: The answer, winter, is never named in the text; the child must use season order to reason that winter sits between fall and spring.

questions.prompt: Read the text: "A turtle carries its shell on its back. When a fox comes near, the turtle pulls its head and legs inside. The hard shell keeps the turtle safe." Why does the turtle pull inside its shell when a fox comes?
choices: Because it wants to take a nap
Because the shell is too heavy to carry
Because the shell keeps it safe from the fox
Because it is looking for food
questions.correct: Because the shell keeps it safe from the fox
questions.hint: Read the first and last sentences together. What does the shell do, and why does that matter when a fox is near?
questions.whyHard: The child must join the fox danger with the final "keeps the turtle safe" line to infer the purpose, not just restate one sentence.

questions.prompt:
Bees live in a hive. They buzz from flower to flower. Bees make sweet honey for us to eat. One hive can hold many bees.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: Flowers
Honey
Hives
Bees
questions.correct: Bees
questions.hint: Look for the one thing that every sentence talks about, not just a word you see one time.
questions.whyHard: Every distractor is a real detail in the text and 'hive'/'honey' feel central, so the child must find the through-line rather than pick the most eye-catching word.

questions.prompt:
It has a soft mane. It runs fast across the grass. Children love to ride on its back. It says 'neigh, neigh!'

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: A dog
A horse
A field
A ride
questions.correct: A horse
questions.hint: Add up all the clues about the animal, then think of which animal fits every one.
questions.whyHard: The main-topic word ('horse') never appears, so the child must infer it by combining several clues (mane, neigh, riding) instead of matching a repeated word.

questions.prompt:
Rain helps plants grow big. It fills up rivers and lakes. Animals drink the water when they are thirsty. Rain is good for the whole earth.

Which sentence tells the BIG main idea?
choices: It fills up rivers and lakes.
Animals drink the water.
Rain is good for the whole earth.
Rain helps plants grow big.
questions.correct: Rain is good for the whole earth.
questions.hint: The main idea is the big one that all the other sentences help explain.
questions.whyHard: Every choice is a true sentence from the text; the child must tell the broad main idea apart from the small details that support it.

questions.prompt:
Some ants dig tunnels under the ground. Other ants build nests up in trees. Ants work together to carry food. Ants are very strong for their small size.

What would be the BEST title for this text?
choices: Ants That Dig
Animals
Carrying Food
All About Ants
questions.correct: All About Ants
questions.hint: A good title is not too small and not too big — it should cover the whole text.
questions.whyHard: Requires rejecting a too-narrow title (one sentence), a too-broad title (all animals), and a single detail, balancing scope to match the whole passage.

questions.prompt:
Sam's little plant looked sad. Its leaves were turning brown. Sam gave the plant water every single day. Soon the leaves were green again!

Why did the leaves turn green?
choices: Sam gave it water
The sun got hot
Sam picked the leaves
The plant was new
questions.correct: Sam gave it water
questions.hint: Find what Sam did right before the leaves changed, even though the text never says 'because.'
questions.whyHard: The cause is never stated with a signal word; the child must connect two separate sentences (giving water, then green again) to infer why.

questions.prompt:
Mia's dog was in the yard. Her baby brother liked to play by the gate. Later, the gate was open and the dog was gone.

Who most likely opened the gate?
choices: Her baby brother
The dog
Mia
The wind
questions.correct: Her baby brother
questions.hint: Look for who was near the gate before it was found open. Match that person to the event.
questions.whyHard: The cause is never stated; the child must connect an individual (the brother by the gate) to a later event (the open gate).

questions.prompt:
Sam shares his crayons with Tom every day. Today Sam had no crayons of his own. Tom gave Sam some of his crayons.

Why did Tom give Sam some crayons?
choices: Sam had always shared with him
Tom had too many crayons
The teacher made him do it
Sam started to cry
questions.correct: Sam had always shared with him
questions.hint: Think about what Sam did for Tom on the other days, and how that might make Tom act back.
questions.whyHard: Requires connecting two individuals across days (Sam's past sharing to Tom's choice today) — an inference about give-and-take, not a stated reason.

questions.prompt:
In fall, the leaves turn colors and drop to the ground. People rake the leaves into big piles. By winter, the trees are bare with no leaves at all.

Why are the trees bare in winter?
choices: The leaves fell off in fall
People raked them off the trees
Snow buried the trees
Birds carried the leaves away
questions.correct: The leaves fell off in fall
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what happened to the leaves before winter came. Connect that event to the bare trees.
questions.whyHard: The child must link an earlier event (leaves dropping in fall) to a later state (bare trees in winter) and reject the closer-in-the-text raking clue.

questions.prompt:
Turtles carry their shells on their backs. When danger comes, a turtle hides inside its shell. A rabbit has no shell, so when danger comes it runs to its hole.

Why does the rabbit run to its hole instead of hiding like the turtle?
choices: It has no shell to hide in
It is faster than a turtle
It is more scared than a turtle
Its hole is warm and soft
questions.correct: It has no shell to hide in
questions.hint: Compare the two animals. What does the turtle have that the rabbit does not, and how does that change what each one does?
questions.whyHard: Two individuals must be connected by contrast — the rabbit's behavior is explained by combining its lack of a shell with the turtle's shell-hiding.

questions.prompt:
Ben's plant sat by the sunny window and grew tall. He moved it to a dark corner for a week. When he looked again, the plant was droopy and short.

What most likely made the plant droop?
choices: It lost its sunlight in the corner
It grew too tall too fast
Ben gave it too much water
The window was too cold
questions.correct: It lost its sunlight in the corner
questions.hint: Notice what changed for the plant between when it was tall and when it drooped. Connect that change to how it looks now.
questions.whyHard: Requires connecting a change in one detail (moving from sun to dark) to a later result (drooping), inferring the sun mattered without it being stated.

questions.prompt: Read this: "The little seeds were dormant all winter. They did not grow. They did not move. When spring came, they finally woke up and sprouted." What does the word 'dormant' mean?
choices: Resting and not growing
Dead and gone forever
Growing very fast
Very cold and wet
questions.correct: Resting and not growing
questions.hint: Look at what the seeds did AFTER winter. Did they stay the same, or did something change?
questions.whyHard: The tempting 'dead and gone' fits 'did not move,' but the child must use 'woke up and sprouted' to rule it out — cross-sentence inference, not a stated definition.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Ben's puppy had not eaten all day. When Ben set down the bowl, the puppy wolfed down its food in one big rush." What does 'wolfed down' mean?
choices: Ate very fast
Howled like a wolf
Hid the food away
Chewed it slowly
questions.correct: Ate very fast
questions.hint: Think about how a puppy that missed meals all day would eat. The word 'rush' is a clue.
questions.whyHard: 'Howled like a wolf' is a literal trap from the word 'wolf'; the answer must come from the clues 'had not eaten all day' and 'one big rush.'

questions.prompt: Read this: "In summer, a big swarm of bees flew over the field. There were so many bees that the sky looked dark. They all buzzed together in one giant cloud." What does the word 'swarm' mean?
choices: A large group flying together
A dark rain cloud
One angry bee
A field of flowers
questions.correct: A large group flying together
questions.hint: The words 'so many' and 'all together' tell you about how many bees there were.
questions.whyHard: 'Dark rain cloud' is baited by 'sky looked dark' and 'giant cloud,' so the child must notice those describe the bees, not weather.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Ants are tiny, but they never work alone. A whole colony shares one home in the dirt. Together they carry a leaf much bigger than any single ant." What does the word 'colony' mean?
choices: A group of ants living together
One very strong ant
A big green leaf
A home made of dirt
questions.correct: A group of ants living together
questions.hint: Ask yourself: does 'colony' name one ant, a place, or many ants? Look at 'whole' and 'together.'
questions.whyHard: 'A home made of dirt' is close because a colony DOES share a home, so the child must separate the group from the place it lives.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Grandpa said his old truck was on its last legs. It broke down almost every week. Soon it would not run at all." What does the phrase 'on its last legs' mean?
choices: Almost worn out and ready to stop
Getting brand new legs
Running faster than ever
Painted a new color
questions.correct: Almost worn out and ready to stop
questions.hint: A truck has no real legs, so use the other clues: it broke down a lot and soon would not run.
questions.whyHard: It is an idiom with no literal legs; 'getting new legs' is the literal-word trap, and the meaning must be built from 'broke down every week' and 'would not run at all.'

questions.prompt: Tim is reading a book about the ocean. He sees the word tide, but he does not know what it means. He wants to find out what the word means. Where in the book should Tim look?
choices: The glossary
The index
The table of contents
The title page
questions.correct: The glossary
questions.hint: You want to know what a word MEANS, not what page it is on. Which part gives the meaning of words?
questions.whyHard: The glossary and the index both list words, so the child must know only the glossary gives meanings.

questions.prompt: Rosa's book has many chapters. She wants to find EVERY page that talks about rain, even pages hidden inside other chapters. Which part of the book will help her most?
choices: The index
The table of contents
The glossary
The cover
questions.correct: The index
questions.hint: You need every single page a topic shows up on, not just where each chapter begins.
questions.whyHard: The table of contents is tempting, but only the index lists every page a topic appears on, not just chapter starts.

questions.prompt: On one page there is a big photo of a spider. Right under the photo is one short line of words. What do those words most likely do?
choices: They tell about the photo
They tell how long the book is
They tell who owns the book
They tell the next chapter
questions.correct: They tell about the photo
questions.hint: Think about where the words are. Words sitting right under a picture usually go with that picture.
questions.whyHard: The job of a caption is not stated; the child must infer it from the words' position under the picture.

questions.prompt: In her science book, Kim sees the word roots printed in thick, dark letters while the words around it are thin. Why did the book make the word roots look different?
choices: To show it is an important word
To show it is spelled wrong
To show the book is ending
To show who wrote the book
questions.correct: To show it is an important word
questions.hint: Books make a word bold on purpose. Ask yourself what a book wants you to notice about that word.
questions.whyHard: The child must infer that bold print is a text feature that flags a key word, not an error or ending.

questions.prompt: A page has the heading Parts of a Plant. It shows a picture of a plant. Small words point to the stem, the leaf, and the roots. What do those small words do?
choices: They name the parts of the plant
They tell what page you are on
They tell the whole book's name
They tell when to read
questions.correct: They name the parts of the plant
questions.hint: The little words point right at pieces of the picture. What would words that point at things be telling you?
questions.whyHard: Labels sit near the heading and picture, so the child must tell labels apart from the heading and page number.

questions.prompt: The words say: "The fox naps in its den." The picture shows a red fox curled up asleep with two baby foxes next to it. Which fact do we learn ONLY from the picture, not from the words?
choices: The fox is napping
The fox is in its den
The fox is red and has two babies
Foxes like to sleep
questions.correct: The fox is red and has two babies
questions.hint: Cross out every fact the words already tell you. What is left can only come from the picture.
questions.whyHard: Two distractors are stated in the words and one is a tempting general guess, so the child must attribute each fact to its source instead of just picking a true statement.

questions.prompt: The picture shows a big gray whale jumping out of blue water. The words say: "A whale is not a fish. It breathes air." Which fact can you learn ONLY from the words, not the picture?
choices: The whale is gray
The whale is big
A whale breathes air
The whale is in water
questions.correct: A whale breathes air
questions.hint: Ask yourself: could a picture ever show this? If your eyes can't see it, it must come from the words.
questions.whyHard: Every wrong choice is something clearly visible in the picture, so the child must realize an invisible fact (breathing) can only live in the words.

questions.prompt: The words say: "Bees make honey in a hive." The picture shows one bee on a yellow flower, far away from any hive. Which fact do BOTH the words and the picture tell you?
choices: Bees make honey
There is a bee
The flower is yellow
Bees live in a hive
questions.correct: There is a bee
questions.hint: Find the one thing that shows up in the words AND in the picture. The others come from just one place.
questions.whyHard: The child must find the single overlapping fact while rejecting facts that are true but come from only one source.

questions.prompt: The words say: "The dog is small." The picture shows a small brown dog wearing a red hat. Tom wants to know what color the dog's hat is. Where should Tom look?
choices: The words, because they describe the dog
The picture, because it shows the hat's color
Both the words and the picture
Neither one tells about the hat
questions.correct: The picture, because it shows the hat's color
questions.hint: Think about which source could answer the hat question. The words talk about the dog, but do they mention a hat?
questions.whyHard: The child must match a specific question to the right source, and the first choice is tempting because the words truly do describe the dog, just not the hat.

questions.prompt: The picture shows a boy in a raincoat holding an umbrella. The words say: "It rained all morning. The boy felt cold." Which fact can you learn ONLY from the words, not the picture?
choices: The boy has an umbrella
The boy wears a raincoat
The boy felt cold
There is an umbrella and a raincoat
questions.correct: The boy felt cold
questions.hint: A picture can show clothes and things, but can it show how someone feels inside?
questions.whyHard: Three distractors are objects visible in the picture, so the child must recognize that a feeling is the one fact a picture cannot show.

questions.prompt:
Bats are helpful animals. They eat bugs that hurt our gardens. Bats sleep upside down in caves.

Which sentence tells WHY bats are helpful?
choices: They eat bugs that hurt our gardens
Bats sleep upside down in caves
Bats are animals
Bats are helpful
questions.correct: They eat bugs that hurt our gardens
questions.hint: A reason answers "why?" Which sentence would finish the sentence "Bats are helpful because ___"?
questions.whyHard: Must separate the true supporting reason from a true-but-unrelated fact and from the point itself.

questions.prompt:
Winter can be fun. You can slide down a snowy hill. Winter is also very cold. You should wear a warm coat so you do not shiver.

What reason does the author give for wearing a warm coat?
choices: So you do not shiver
So you can slide down the hill
Because winter is fun
Because snow is white
questions.correct: So you do not shiver
questions.hint: Find the point the coat sentence goes with. Is a coat about the FUN part or the COLD part?
questions.whyHard: Two points are made; the reason must be matched to the correct point (cold) while a distractor fits the other point (fun).

questions.prompt:
Milk is good for kids. It makes your bones strong. It also helps your teeth grow well.

The author gives these reasons to show that ____.
choices: milk is good for kids
milk is white
teeth are important
bones can break
questions.correct: milk is good for kids
questions.hint: The two reasons both back up one big idea. What is that big idea?
questions.whyHard: Reverses the skill: the child must find the point the reasons support instead of restating a reason.

questions.prompt:
You should wash your hands before you eat. Clean hands keep germs away.

Which sentence gives ANOTHER good reason to wash your hands?
choices: Clean hands help keep you from getting sick
Your hands have ten fingers
The soap is a nice color
Water comes out of the sink
questions.correct: Clean hands help keep you from getting sick
questions.hint: A reason has to tell why washing hands is a good idea, not just say something about hands or soap.
questions.whyHard: Application: the child must judge which new fact actually supports the point, not just which is on-topic.

questions.prompt:
Owls hunt at night. Their big eyes help them see in the dark. Their soft feathers let them fly so quietly that mice never hear them coming.

Why can an owl sneak up on a mouse without being heard?
choices: Its soft feathers let it fly quietly
Its big eyes help it see in the dark
Owls hunt at night
Mice are very small
questions.correct: Its soft feathers let it fly quietly
questions.hint: The author gives two different reasons. Pick the one about being QUIET, not the one about seeing.
questions.whyHard: Two reasons support two sub-points; the child must attach the right reason to the specific point (being unheard, not seeing).

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Frogs hop. Frogs live by ponds."
Book 2: "Frogs are green. Frogs live by ponds."
What is the ONE thing BOTH books tell you about frogs?
choices: Frogs hop.
Frogs are green.
Frogs live by ponds.
Frogs eat bugs.
questions.correct: Frogs live by ponds.
questions.hint: A true fact for both books must show up in BOTH books, not just one. Check each choice against both.
questions.whyHard: Both books are true, so every choice sounds right; the child must cross-check both texts to find the ONE detail they share.

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Bats fly at night."
Book 2: "Bats fly in the morning."
The two books do NOT agree about one thing. What is it?
choices: That bats fly
WHEN bats fly
Where bats sleep
What bats eat
questions.correct: WHEN bats fly
questions.hint: Both books say bats fly, so that part matches. Look for the word that is different in each book.
questions.whyHard: The books agree AND disagree in the same sentence; the child must separate the shared idea (flying) from the mismatched detail (the time).

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Bees make honey. Bees have wings."
Book 2: "Bees have wings. Bees can sting."
Which fact is in Book 2 but NOT in Book 1?
choices: Bees can sting.
Bees have wings.
Bees make honey.
Bees swim in water.
questions.correct: Bees can sting.
questions.hint: Cross out any fact that shows up in Book 1. What is left is only in Book 2.
questions.whyHard: Requires comparing two fact lists and finding the item unique to one book, while a shared fact (wings) and a Book-1-only fact both sit as traps.

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Rain comes down from the clouds."
Book 2: "On a rainy day, wear boots and a coat."
You want to know what to put on before you go outside in the rain. Which book helps you MORE?
choices: Book 1
Book 2
Both books help the same
Neither book helps
questions.correct: Book 2
questions.hint: Think about what each book is really about. One tells where rain comes from; the other tells what to do about rain.
questions.whyHard: The child must figure out each book's purpose, then match a new real-life goal to the right one — a novel application, not a stated answer.

questions.prompt:
Book 1: "Ants are the best bugs! They work so hard."
Book 2: "Ants are just okay. They bite you."
How are the two books different?
choices: Book 1 likes ants more than Book 2 does.
Book 2 likes ants more than Book 1 does.
Both books think ants are the best.
Both books say ants can fly.
questions.correct: Book 1 likes ants more than Book 2 does.
questions.hint: Listen to the happy or grumpy feeling in each book's words, not just the facts. Which book sounds like it likes ants?
questions.whyHard: The difference is in each author's feeling/tone ("best!" vs "just okay"), so the child must infer opinion instead of matching plain facts.

questions.prompt:
Every morning, Mrs. Lee puts a dish of seeds on her back porch. Soon little birds fly down to eat. But when her cat sits by the door, no birds come at all. So Mrs. Lee keeps the cat inside during breakfast time.

Why does Mrs. Lee keep the cat inside in the morning?
choices: So the cat can sleep a little longer
So the birds are not scared away
So the cat will not eat the seeds
So the porch stays nice and clean
questions.correct: So the birds are not scared away
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells what happens when the cat is by the door. What does that clue tell you about why she moves the cat?
questions.whyHard: The reason is never stated; the child must link 'cat by door = no birds' to infer the cause.

questions.prompt:
Sam plants a bean seed and watches it grow. First, a tiny root grows down into the soil. Next, a green stem pushes up. After many days, small leaves open at the top of the stem. Last of all, a flower appears.

When do the leaves open?
choices: Before the stem grows up
Right after the root grows down
After the stem grows but before the flower
At the very end, after the flower
questions.correct: After the stem grows but before the flower
questions.hint: Put the four things in order. Then find where the leaves fit compared to the stem and the flower.
questions.whyHard: Requires ordering four sequenced events and placing one between two others, not just naming a single step.

questions.prompt:
Ants are very strong for their size. When one ant finds a big crumb, it cannot carry the crumb alone. So the ant runs back to the nest and taps other ants with its feelers. Then many ants come and carry the crumb home together.

Why does the ant tap the other ants with its feelers?
choices: To say hello to its friends
To ask them to help carry the crumb
To warn them that danger is near
To show them the way back home
questions.correct: To ask them to help carry the crumb
questions.hint: Look at what happens right before and right after the tapping. What problem is the ant trying to solve?
questions.whyHard: The purpose of the tapping is implied by the surrounding sentences, not spelled out.

questions.prompt:
Our class has three helpers each day. The line leader walks in front when the class moves. The door holder keeps the door open for everyone. The light monitor turns off the lights when the class leaves the room. Today, Maya is the very last one to do her job.

What is Maya's job today?
choices: Line leader
Door holder
Light monitor
Teacher's helper
questions.correct: Light monitor
questions.hint: Which job happens LAST, when the class is already leaving the room?
questions.whyHard: Child must match 'last one to do her job' to the role whose task happens last, reasoning across two clues.

questions.prompt:
Deep down in the sea, some fish make their own light. They live so far down that sunlight from above cannot reach them. Their glowing light helps them find food and see each other.

What can we tell about the deep sea from this text?
choices: It is warm and full of sunshine
It is dark because sunlight cannot reach it
It has many green plants growing
It is right near the top of the water
questions.correct: It is dark because sunlight cannot reach it
questions.hint: Why would a fish need to make its OWN light? Use that clue to picture what the deep sea is like.
questions.whyHard: The word 'dark' is never used; the child infers it from 'sunlight cannot reach' plus 'make their own light.'

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Beavers cut down trees with their strong front teeth. They drag the branches into a river and stack them until the water stops flowing. This makes a deep, calm pond behind the pile of wood. Beavers build their homes in the middle of these ponds. The deep water keeps hungry wolves and bears from reaching them.

Why do beavers stack branches until the water stops flowing?
choices: To make deep water that keeps them safe
To have extra branches to eat later
To clear the trees away from the river
To help wolves and bears cross the river
questions.correct: To make deep water that keeps them safe
questions.hint: Follow the chain: stopping the water makes a pond, and the last sentence tells you what the deep water does.
questions.whyHard: Requires linking three sentences (stack wood -> deep pond -> keeps predators away) instead of grabbing one stated fact.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Some frogs freeze almost solid in winter. Their hearts stop beating and they do not breathe. To an animal that finds one, the frog seems dead. But sugar in the frog's body keeps the inside of its cells from turning hard. When spring warms the ground, the frog thaws out and hops away.

What is the sugar's most important job for the frog?
choices: It protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard
It keeps the frog's heart beating all winter
It makes the frog taste bad to other animals
It warms the frog up when spring comes
questions.correct: It protects the inside of the frog's cells from freezing hard
questions.hint: Find the sentence that says what the sugar does, and notice the word 'But' before it.
questions.whyHard: Distractors reuse true details from nearby sentences (heart stops, spring warms), so the child must pin the effect to the sugar specifically.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Long ago, sailors could not tell how far east or west they were. Many ships got lost and crashed on rocks. Then a clockmaker built a clock that kept perfect time even on a rocking ship. By comparing the clock's time to the sun, sailors finally knew their place on the sea. After that, far fewer ships were lost.

What happened because the clockmaker built his special clock?
choices: Fewer ships got lost and crashed
Ships could sail faster than before
Sailors no longer needed to watch the sun
The sea became calmer for the ships
questions.correct: Fewer ships got lost and crashed
questions.hint: Read to the very end. What changed for the ships after the clock was invented?
questions.whyHard: The cause and its result are separated by two sentences; one distractor (no longer need the sun) contradicts how the clock actually worked.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

A cactus lives where rain almost never falls. Instead of flat leaves, it has sharp spines, so very little water leaks out into the dry air. Its thick stem swells up like a sponge to store water from one rare rain. Roots spread out just under the sand to soak up a shower fast, before the sun dries the ground.

Why does having spines instead of flat leaves help the cactus?
choices: It loses less water into the dry air
It can store more water in its roots
It can soak up rain more quickly
It can grow taller in the sand
questions.correct: It loses less water into the dry air
questions.hint: The sentence about spines has a clue word 'so' that tells you what the spines do.
questions.whyHard: Every choice is a real cactus water strategy from the passage; the child must match the benefit to the spines and not to the stem or roots.

questions.prompt:
Read the passage.

Honey bees dance to talk. When a bee finds flowers full of food, it flies home and dances in a circle. The other bees crowd close and feel the dance with their feelers. Then they fly straight out to find the same flowers. A bee that finds no food does not dance at all.

What can you tell about a hive where the bees are NOT dancing?
choices: The bees have not found flowers with food
The bees are too tired to fly
The bees are dancing in a different way
The bees have too much food to eat
questions.correct: The bees have not found flowers with food
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you when a bee will not dance. Use that rule backwards.
questions.whyHard: Requires reversing a stated rule (found food -> dances) to infer the meaning of no dancing, rather than reading an answer straight off the page.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

Bees live together in a hive with one queen bee. Bees fly from flower to flower and use the sweet juice to make honey. When they land on flowers, they move yellow dust called pollen, and this helps new plants grow.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: Where the queen bee lives in the hive
How bees turn flower juice into honey
The important jobs that bees do
How bees help new plants grow
questions.correct: The important jobs that bees do
questions.hint: One choice covers the WHOLE text. The others are each true, but only tell about one small part.
questions.whyHard: Three distractors are all TRUE details, each covering only one sentence; the child must pick the umbrella topic that fits all of them, not the narrowest true fact.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph.

Penguins cannot fly the way other birds do. But in the water they are amazing. Their wings work like flippers to push them fast, and they can dive deep down to catch fish.

What is this paragraph MOSTLY about?
choices: Penguins cannot fly like other birds
How penguins are great swimmers
The kinds of fish that penguins eat
Why penguins have wings
questions.correct: How penguins are great swimmers
questions.hint: The first sentence sets up a surprise. Read the whole paragraph to find what it spends the most time telling you.
questions.whyHard: The tempting distractor is the literal first sentence, but the paragraph's actual focus is the contrast that follows; the child must weigh the whole paragraph over the opening line.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

Rain forests are hot, and it rains there almost every day. So many animals live in them, like tiny bright frogs and huge, long snakes. Rain forests also give people things we use, like fruit to eat and plants that become medicine.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: The huge snakes that live in rain forests
Why it rains so much in rain forests
What rain forests are like and why they matter
The medicines that come from rain forest plants
questions.correct: What rain forests are like and why they matter
questions.hint: A cool, exciting detail is not the same as the main topic. Ask what the text is about from start to finish.
questions.whyHard: The vivid 'huge snakes' detail pulls attention, and two other choices are real facts from single sentences; only the broad choice captures every paragraph.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph.

Long ago, people had no clocks. They looked up at the sun to know the time. When the sun was high in the sky, it was the middle of the day. When the sun went down, they knew it was time to sleep.

What is this paragraph MOSTLY about?
choices: Why people feel sleepy at night
How the sun moves across the sky each day
How people long ago used the sun to tell time
Why people needed to build the first clocks
questions.correct: How people long ago used the sun to tell time
questions.hint: Several choices mention something the paragraph touches. Find the one big idea that ties every sentence together.
questions.whyHard: Each distractor borrows a real word or hint from the text (sleep, the moving sun, clocks) so all feel plausible; the child must synthesize across sentences to the shared purpose.

questions.prompt:
Read the text.

A librarian's day begins early. She turns on the lights and unlocks the big doors. All morning and afternoon she helps kids find books they will love. Before the library closes, she reads a story out loud so everyone can listen together.

What is this text MOSTLY about?
choices: How a librarian opens the library in the morning
The different ways a librarian helps at the library
Why kids love to check out library books
How to find a good book on the shelf
questions.correct: The different ways a librarian helps at the library
questions.hint: Don't just grab the topic of the very first sentence. Notice what she does across the WHOLE day.
questions.whyHard: Kids commonly mistake the first paragraph's topic (opening up in the morning) for the whole-text topic; the correct answer requires seeing the pattern across the full day.

questions.prompt:
A seed needs water to grow. When it rains, the ground gets wet, and the wet ground helps a seed sprout into a tiny plant. But when there is no rain for many weeks, the ground turns dry and hard, and the seeds just stay under the dirt.

Why do the seeds stay under the dirt when it does not rain for weeks?
choices: The dry ground has no water the seeds need
The wet ground is too soft for them
The seeds are too old to grow anymore
Birds carried the seeds away in the rain
questions.correct: The dry ground has no water the seeds need
questions.hint: Find the one thing the seeds need in the first sentence, then check what dry ground is missing.
questions.whyHard: Must link 'seeds need water' to 'no rain makes dry ground' across separate sentences to infer a cause the text never states directly.

questions.prompt:
To make a paper boat, Mia first folds the paper in half. Next, she folds the corners down to make a pointed top. Then she folds up the bottom edges to make the two sides. Last, she gently pulls the two sides open so the boat can float.

Why must Mia pull the sides open LAST and not first?
choices: The sides must be folded before they can be opened
Pulling too early would make the paper wet
The corners cannot be folded once the boat is open
The paper is easier to hold when it is flat
questions.correct: The sides must be folded before they can be opened
questions.hint: Look at which earlier step actually makes the sides. Can you open something that isn't there yet?
questions.whyHard: Requires reasoning about why the step order matters, inferring that one step depends on an earlier step finishing first.

questions.prompt:
Long ago, people had to walk or ride horses to travel, and it took a long time. Then trains were built, and people could go much farther in less time. Later, cars were invented, so families could leave right from their own homes and travel whenever they wanted.

What did cars let people do that trains did NOT?
choices: Leave from their own homes whenever they wanted
Travel slower than a walking horse
Ride only along the train tracks
Travel before trains were even built
questions.correct: Leave from their own homes whenever they wanted
questions.hint: Compare the train sentence with the car sentence, and find the new thing only cars added.
questions.whyHard: Must contrast two events in the series and pull out the specific new detail cars added, instead of picking the true-but-shared idea of faster travel.

questions.prompt:
Bees fly from flower to flower to drink sweet nectar. As a bee lands, yellow dust called pollen sticks to its fuzzy legs. When the bee flies to the next flower, some of that pollen rubs off. Flowers need pollen from other flowers before they can make new seeds.

Why are bees important to flowers?
choices: They carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds
They drink up all the nectar so the flowers stay dry
They eat the pollen off of every flower they land on
They dig the flowers' seeds down into the ground
questions.correct: They carry pollen between flowers so the flowers can make seeds
questions.hint: Follow the pollen: where does it stick, where does it go, and what do flowers need it for?
questions.whyHard: Requires chaining three separate facts (pollen sticks, rubs off at the next flower, flowers need other flowers' pollen) to build a cause the text never spells out.

questions.prompt:
Sam planted a bean seed in a cup of dirt on Monday, and he watered it every single day. He did not see anything happen at first. Then on Friday, Sam noticed a tiny green stem poking up out of the dirt, so he kept watering it.

What most likely happened to the seed between Monday and Friday?
choices: It slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt
It turned back into a bean for Sam to eat
It grew tall enough to open up its first flowers
It stayed exactly the same until Sam added new dirt
questions.correct: It slowly soaked up water and began to sprout under the dirt
questions.hint: You see the start (a seed) and the end (a tiny stem). Think about what step must have happened in between, hidden in the dirt.
questions.whyHard: Must infer the unstated middle of a process from only its start and end states, while rejecting a distractor that jumps too far ahead in the sequence.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about deserts:

"The desert gets almost no rain. To survive, a cactus stores water deep inside its thick stem. On the hottest days, when other plants wilt and die, the cactus stays green."

What does the word **stores** mean here?
choices: Sells things to people
Keeps something to use later
Buys water from a shop
Pours something out quickly
questions.correct: Keeps something to use later
questions.hint: Read the whole paragraph. Why would a cactus hold water inside its stem in a place with almost no rain? Use the clues about surviving the hottest days.
questions.whyHard: 'Stores' has a common everyday meaning (a shop) that traps readers; the correct sense must be inferred from the survival context, not stated.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a science book:

"Bees are busy helpers in a garden. As a bee crawls into a flower to drink, yellow dust sticks to its legs. The bee carries this dust to the next flower. This is how bees pollinate the plants so new seeds can grow."

What does **pollinate** mean in this paragraph?
choices: Drink sweet juice from a flower
Move flower dust so seeds can grow
Build a home inside a flower
Sting animals that come too close
questions.correct: Move flower dust so seeds can grow
questions.hint: The word comes right after the bee carries dust from flower to flower. Match the meaning to what the bee is DOING, not just what it eats.
questions.whyHard: A tempting distractor ('drink juice') restates an earlier true detail; the reader must track which action the word names across two sentences.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a report about the ocean:

"Deep in the sea it is pitch black. No sunlight reaches down that far. Yet some tiny fish make their own light. The glowing spots help them find each other in the dark."

What does **pitch black** mean here?
choices: Full of bright light
Covered in sticky glue
Completely dark
A little bit cloudy
questions.correct: Completely dark
questions.hint: Look at the two sentences right after the phrase. What do they tell you about the light down there? Let those clues decide the meaning.
questions.whyHard: 'Pitch' as a separate word (glue/tar) baits a wrong pick; only combining the 'no sunlight' and 'find each other in the dark' clues gives the meaning.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about weather:

"After days of rain, the little creek began to swell. The water climbed over its banks and spread across the road. Soon cars could not drive through."

What does the word **swell** mean in this sentence?
choices: Get smaller and dry up
Get bigger and rise up
Turn a bright blue color
Move very slowly
questions.correct: Get bigger and rise up
questions.hint: Don't guess from the word alone. Read what happens next to the water, then pick the meaning that fits that whole picture.
questions.whyHard: The answer isn't defined; it must be inferred by chaining the next two sentences (water climbing over banks, spreading) into cause and effect.

questions.prompt:
Read this from a book about the human body:

"When you run fast, your heart pumps harder. You breathe quickly, and your skin gets warm. Then your body sweats. The little drops of water cool you down so you don't get too hot."

What does the word **sweats** mean here?
choices: Runs faster and faster
Makes drops of water to cool the body
Turns a warm red color
Takes a long, slow breath
questions.correct: Makes drops of water to cool the body
questions.hint: The sentence right after the word explains what the drops do. Use that clue instead of the other things the body does when you run.
questions.whyHard: Three distractors each echo a real detail from the passage (running, warm skin, breathing), so the reader must isolate which one the word actually names.

questions.prompt:
Here is the Table of Contents for a book about weather:

Chapter 1: What Is Weather? ... page 2
Chapter 2: Clouds and Rain ... page 8
Chapter 3: Storms ... page 14
Chapter 4: Weather Tools ... page 20

Maria wants to learn how a rain gauge measures how much water falls. Which chapter should she turn to first?
choices: Chapter 4: Weather Tools
Chapter 2: Clouds and Rain
Chapter 3: Storms
Chapter 1: What Is Weather?
questions.correct: Chapter 4: Weather Tools
questions.hint: Think about WHAT a rain gauge is, not just the word 'rain.' Match the idea to the chapter, not the matching word.
questions.whyHard: The word 'rain' pulls the child to Chapter 2, but a rain gauge is a tool, so they must match the idea (a measuring tool) instead of the keyword.

questions.prompt:
Here is part of the Index from a bug book:

ants ... 4, 9
bees ... 7, 12
spiders ... 3, 15, 22
webs ... 15

Sam wants ONE page that tells about spiders AND their webs together. Which page should he open?
choices: Page 15
Page 3
Page 22
Page 7
questions.correct: Page 15
questions.hint: Look at the page numbers listed for 'spiders' and the page numbers listed for 'webs.' Find the number that shows up in BOTH lists.
questions.whyHard: The child must cross-reference two separate index entries and notice that page 15 is the only number shared by both, rather than picking any spider page.

questions.prompt: While reading, you see a word printed in dark, thick letters: **migrate**. You do not know what it means and want to find out as fast as you can. What is the BEST thing to do?
choices: Look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained
Look up 'migrate' in the index to see the page numbers it is on
Read the whole chapter over again from the start
Look at the pictures on the page and guess
questions.correct: Look up 'migrate' in the glossary, where bold words are explained
questions.hint: You want the MEANING of the word. Think about which text feature gives meanings, and remember what bold letters usually signal.
questions.whyHard: The index is a tempting text-feature distractor, but it only gives page numbers; the child must know a bold word signals a glossary entry, which gives the actual meaning.

questions.prompt:
A page in a book has these headings:

- Where Sea Turtles Live
- What Sea Turtles Eat
- How Baby Sea Turtles Grow
- Why Sea Turtles Are in Danger

Max wants to find out how sea turtles catch the fish they hunt for food. Which section should he read?
choices: What Sea Turtles Eat
Where Sea Turtles Live
How Baby Sea Turtles Grow
Why Sea Turtles Are in Danger
questions.correct: What Sea Turtles Eat
questions.hint: The heading may not use the exact words in the question. Decide what BIG idea 'catching fish for food' belongs to, then match it.
questions.whyHard: 'Fish' and 'hunt' hint at 'Where Sea Turtles Live,' but catching food is about eating, so the child must infer the topic instead of matching surface words.

questions.prompt: A book about planets has BOTH a table of contents in the front and an index in the back. Rosa wants to find EVERY page that has the word 'Mars' on it. Which one will help her find all of those pages faster?
choices: The index, because it lists a word and every page it appears on
The table of contents, because it lists the chapters in order
The glossary, because it tells what the word 'Mars' means
The title page, because it shows the name of the book
questions.correct: The index, because it lists a word and every page it appears on
questions.hint: You want every single page where one exact word shows up. Think about which list is sorted by words, not by chapters.
questions.whyHard: Children often confuse the table of contents and the index; the child must reason that only the index maps a single word to all of its page numbers efficiently.

questions.prompt:
Have you ever watched a spider fix its web? First, the spider makes one strong line between two branches. Then it adds more lines that spread out like the spokes of a wheel. Last, it spins a sticky circle to catch bugs. Now you know how a spider builds its web!

What is the author's MAIN purpose?
choices: To explain the steps a spider takes to build a web
To describe what a spider looks like
To tell an exciting story about a spider and a bug
To convince you that spiders are helpful
questions.correct: To explain the steps a spider takes to build a web
questions.hint: Look at how the text is built: first, then, last. What are those words showing you?
questions.whyHard: The 'describe' and 'story' choices are tempting because the text opens like a story, but the first/then/last order signals an explain-the-steps purpose.

questions.prompt:
Why do leaves change color in the fall? In summer, leaves are full of green stuff that helps the tree make food. When the days get short and cold, that green stuff fades away. Then we can finally see the red and orange colors that were hiding all along.

The author wrote this text mostly to —
choices: Answer why leaves change color in the fall
Explain how a tree makes its food in summer
Describe how pretty the red and orange leaves are
Tell a story about a walk on a fall day
questions.correct: Answer why leaves change color in the fall
questions.hint: Two of the choices are true details from the text. Which one is the big question the whole text is answering?
questions.whyHard: 'How a tree makes food' is stated right in the text, so it feels correct — but it is a supporting detail, not the main question the author set out to answer.

questions.prompt:
Recess is the best part of the school day! Kids need time to run and play so their bodies stay strong. Playing outside also helps kids think better when they go back to class. Every school should give kids a long recess.

What is the author trying to do?
choices: Convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess
Inform readers about how the body stays strong
Explain the rules of the games kids play at recess
Describe what a school playground looks like
questions.correct: Convince readers that schools should give kids a long recess
questions.hint: Some sentences give facts, but notice words like 'best' and 'should.' What does the author want you to believe?
questions.whyHard: The middle sentences really do inform, making the 'inform about the body' choice tempting, but the 'best' and 'every school should' frame reveals a persuade purpose.

questions.prompt:
The rainforest is loud and green. Tall trees block the sun, so the ground below stays dark and wet. Bright birds call from the high branches, and frogs sing near the little streams. Everywhere you look, something is moving.

The author mainly wants to —
choices: Describe what the rainforest is like
Explain why the trees grow so tall
Teach readers how to take care of frogs
Convince readers to visit the rainforest
questions.correct: Describe what the rainforest is like
questions.hint: Is the author giving you reasons WHY things happen, or painting a picture so you can imagine the place?
questions.whyHard: 'Tall trees block the sun' looks like a why-explanation, pulling toward the 'explain' choice, but the whole text paints a sensory picture — a describe purpose, not an explain one.

questions.prompt:
Some people think bats are birds because they fly. But bats are not birds at all — they are mammals, just like dogs and people. Bats have soft fur instead of feathers, and mother bats feed their babies milk.

Why did the author write this?
choices: To correct the idea that bats are birds
To explain how dogs and people are alike
To describe what baby bats look like
To tell readers where bats like to sleep
questions.correct: To correct the idea that bats are birds
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you a wrong idea some people have. What does the rest of the text do with that wrong idea?
questions.whyHard: The comparison to dogs and people is vivid, tempting the reader to pick it, but every detail exists to fix the mistaken 'bats are birds' belief — the reader must see the corrective purpose behind the facts.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Bats are helpful animals. One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests on their crops. Some people are scared of bats. But bats almost never bother people, and they would rather fly away than come close." The author wants you to believe that bats are HELPFUL. Which sentence gives a reason for THAT point?
choices: Some people are scared of bats.
One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests.
Bats almost never bother people.
Bats would rather fly away than come close.
questions.correct: One bat can eat over a thousand bugs in one night, so farmers have fewer pests.
questions.hint: The passage makes two different points. Find the reason that matches the point about bats being helpful, not the point about bats being safe.
questions.whyHard: Two points share one passage; the child must match a reason to the correct point instead of grabbing any true bat fact.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Kids should walk to school when they can. Walking gives your body exercise before the day even starts. It also means one less car on the road, so the air stays cleaner." Why does having one less car help keep the air cleaner?
choices: Because cars are faster than walking.
Because cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke.
Because walking gives your body exercise.
Because kids like to walk with their friends.
questions.correct: Because cars put dirty smoke into the air, so fewer cars means less smoke.
questions.hint: The author never spells out WHY fewer cars help. Use the clue "one less car... so the air stays cleaner" to figure out what cars must be doing to the air.
questions.whyHard: The child must infer the unstated link (cars make smoke) that connects the reason to the point about cleaner air.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Libraries are great places for kids. At the library, you can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want. Libraries are usually very quiet. Some libraries were built a long, long time ago." Which sentence best supports the point that libraries are GREAT for kids?
choices: Libraries are usually very quiet.
You can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want.
Some libraries were built a long, long time ago.
Libraries are great places for kids.
questions.correct: You can borrow any book for free, so you can read as many stories as you want.
questions.hint: Every choice is true, but only one gives a REASON that shows why libraries are great for kids. Watch out for facts that are just interesting and for a choice that only repeats the point.
questions.whyHard: Several statements are true; the child must tell a supporting reason apart from a neutral fact and from a restatement of the claim itself.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Read the label before you buy a snack. The label tells you how much sugar is inside. It also shows if there is something that might make you sick, like peanuts." Why does the author mention that the label shows peanuts?
choices: To show that peanuts taste good.
To give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe.
To show that labels are hard to read.
To tell you that all snacks are bad for you.
questions.correct: To give a reason that reading the label can keep you safe.
questions.hint: Ask what job the peanut detail is doing. How does knowing about peanuts help with the author's big point about reading the label?
questions.whyHard: The child must connect one small detail (peanuts) to the author's larger point by naming its role as a supporting reason.

questions.prompt: Read this: "Dogs make good pets for families. A dog will play with children for hours. A dog can also learn to sit and stay when you teach it." The author wants to add ONE more reason that dogs make good pets. Which sentence should the author add?
choices: Dogs need to visit the vet once every year.
A dog will bark loudly all through the night.
A dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely.
Dogs come in many different sizes and colors.
questions.correct: A dog will keep your family company so no one feels lonely.
questions.hint: A new reason has to make the author's point stronger, not weaker. Which sentence gives a real reason dogs are GOOD pets, instead of a plain fact or a downside?
questions.whyHard: The child must judge which brand-new fact would actually work as a reason for the point, rejecting true statements that are neutral or that argue against it.

questions.prompt:
Two short texts about bees.

Text A: Bees help flowers grow. When a bee lands on a flower to drink, yellow dust sticks to its legs. The bee carries that dust to the next flower. This helps the flower make seeds.

Text B: Bees are busy workers. A bee flies from flower to flower all day. It carries sweet food back to its hive to feed the baby bees.

Both texts tell you that a bee ____.
choices: moves from one flower to another
makes seeds for the flower
feeds the baby bees in the hive
carries yellow dust on its legs
questions.correct: moves from one flower to another
questions.hint: Cross out any idea that only ONE text mentions. Keep the idea both texts say in their own words.
questions.whyHard: The shared point is worded differently in each text, and three distractors are true statements pulled from only one text.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about rain.

Text A: Rain is good for us. It fills the rivers we drink from and helps the crops on farms grow tall.

Text B: Too much rain can be a problem. When rain falls hard for many days, rivers can flood and cover the roads.

How are the two texts DIFFERENT?
choices: Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble
Both texts say rain is dangerous
Text A is about rivers, and Text B is about the sun
Text A says rain floods roads, but Text B says rain fills rivers
questions.correct: Text A says rain helps, but Text B says rain can cause trouble
questions.hint: Ask what MAIN point each text is making about rain, then find the choice that matches each one correctly.
questions.whyHard: One distractor swaps which text says what, testing whether the child tracks each text's point separately.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about penguins.

Text A: Penguins have wings, but they cannot fly. They use their wings like paddles to swim fast and catch fish in the cold water.

Text B: Penguins stay warm in the freezing cold. Their thick feathers and a layer of fat keep the icy water away from their skin.

Both texts help you understand that penguins ____.
choices: live where it is cold and near water
cannot use their wings to fly
have thick feathers to stay warm
paddle with their wings to swim
questions.correct: live where it is cold and near water
questions.hint: Look for the idea you can find clues for in BOTH texts, even if neither says it in those exact words.
questions.whyHard: The correct answer is inferred from clues in both texts (cold water, icy water, fish), while each distractor comes from only one text and states it directly.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about a class garden.

Text A: Our class grew tomatoes. We watered the plants every morning, and after many weeks the red tomatoes were ready to pick.

Text B: Our class grew sunflowers. We gave them water each day, and the tall yellow flowers took the whole spring to bloom.

What is ONE way both classes' plants were the same?
choices: Both plants needed water often to grow
Both classes grew red tomatoes
Both plants grew tall in one week
Both plants were bright yellow
questions.correct: Both plants needed water often to grow
questions.hint: Find something that is true in Text A AND true in Text B. Two of the choices are only true for one text.
questions.whyHard: Requires matching a shared detail (watering) across texts while rejecting distractors that are each true for only one text.

questions.prompt:
Two texts about lunch at school.

Text A: Maya thinks packing lunch is best. You get to choose your own food, and you never have to wait in a long line.

Text B: Sam thinks buying lunch is best. The food is warm, and you do not have to carry a heavy bag to school.

What do Maya and Sam DISAGREE about?
choices: Whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice
Whether lunch lines are too long
Whether school food is warm enough
Whether lunch bags are too heavy
questions.correct: Whether packing or buying lunch is the better choice
questions.hint: A disagreement is the BIG question both writers answer differently, not just one small reason.
questions.whyHard: The distractors are each a single reason from one text; the child must see that the real disagreement is the overall point both are arguing.

questions.prompt:
Maya's class wanted a garden, but they had no money and no tools. First, they sold their old books to earn some cash. Then each student brought one small shovel or rake from home. On Saturday, teachers AND parents came to help them dig. By spring, the garden was bursting with plants.

What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: The whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen
Maya's class sold their old books to earn money
Spring is the best time to grow plants
Teachers and parents like gardens
questions.correct: The whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen
questions.hint: The main idea is what ALL the sentences add up to, not just one thing that happened. Ask what the whole story is really about.
questions.whyHard: The main idea is never stated; the reader must synthesize four separate steps into one theme and reject a true-but-too-narrow single detail.

questions.prompt:
Owls are built to hunt in the dark of night. Their huge eyes soak up even the tiniest bit of moonlight. The author also tells us that owls have soft, fluffy feathers.

How does the detail about soft feathers help support the main idea?
choices: Soft feathers let owls fly silently, so they can sneak up on prey at night
Soft feathers keep owls warm on cold nights
Soft feathers make owls look pretty and fluffy
Soft feathers help owls build better nests
questions.correct: Soft feathers let owls fly silently, so they can sneak up on prey at night
questions.hint: A detail supports the main idea only if it connects back to it. The main idea here is about HUNTING at night, so ask which choice ties feathers to hunting.
questions.whyHard: Every choice states something plausibly true about feathers; the reader must pick the one that links the detail to the specific main idea (night hunting), not just any real fact about feathers.

questions.prompt:
The Nile River gave ancient Egypt almost everything it needed. Its water let farmers grow wheat in the middle of a dry desert. When it flooded, it left behind thick mud that made rich soil for crops. Boats carried food and goods up and down the river so people could trade.

Which sentence best states the main idea?
choices: The Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt
Rivers are found in many places around the world
Farmers in Egypt grew wheat
Boats can carry goods from place to place
questions.correct: The Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt
questions.hint: A good main idea is not too big and not too small. Cross out any choice that is way broader than the passage OR that is just one small detail from it.
questions.whyHard: The reader must reject a too-broad choice (all rivers everywhere) and two too-narrow single details, keeping only the 'just right' main idea that covers the whole passage.

questions.prompt:
Penguins have clever ways to stay warm in the freezing cold. They huddle together in huge groups so their bodies block the icy wind. They also have a thick layer of fat under their skin that holds in heat.

Which NEW sentence would best support the main idea of this passage?
choices: Their feathers are packed tightly together to trap warm air against their skin
Penguins cannot fly up into the air like other birds
Penguins dive into the sea to catch fish to eat
Baby penguins are covered in soft gray fluff
questions.correct: Their feathers are packed tightly together to trap warm air against their skin
questions.hint: The main idea is about how penguins STAY WARM. A supporting sentence must be about warmth, even if the other sentences are also true about penguins.
questions.whyHard: This is a novel-application task: all four choices are true facts about penguins, so the reader must judge each against the main idea and keep only the one about staying warm.

questions.prompt:
Many people think spiders are creepy pests they should squash. But spiders actually help us in ways we hardly notice. They catch flies and mosquitoes that carry germs. They eat the bugs that would chew holes in our garden plants. Some farmers even count on spiders to protect their crops.

What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Spiders are actually helpful to people
Spiders are creepy pests that people should squash
Mosquitoes carry germs that can make people sick
Farmers work hard to grow their crops
questions.correct: Spiders are actually helpful to people
questions.hint: Watch the word 'But' after the first sentence. Sometimes the first line says what people WRONGLY think, and the real main idea is the opposite.
questions.whyHard: The opening sentence states the opposite of the main idea, tempting the reader to pick it; the true main idea is signaled by 'But' and proven by the details, requiring the reader to override the most prominent stated claim.

questions.prompt:
Read this science passage:

"Salmon are strong swimmers. When it is time to lay their eggs, they must travel up the river to the place where they were born. Even when the river's current pushes hard against them, the salmon keep swimming forward and never give up."

What does the word **current** mean in this passage?
choices: The flow of water moving in a direction
Electricity that powers a light
Something that is happening right now
The newest and most modern kind
questions.correct: The flow of water moving in a direction
questions.hint: The word has more than one meaning. Look at what the current DOES to the salmon in the river to pick the right one.
questions.whyHard: 'Current' most commonly means 'happening now' or 'electricity' to a child; the reader must reject those familiar meanings and infer the water-flow meaning from context.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"In winter, a bear does not die. It curls up in its cave for a long, deep sleep. Its heartbeat slows down, and it does not eat a single bite for many months. Scientists say the bear stays dormant until spring warms the air."

What does **dormant** mean here?
choices: Resting and not active
Dead and gone forever
Very hungry and hunting for food
Awake and moving around the cave
questions.correct: Resting and not active
questions.hint: Each wrong answer matches ONE small detail. Find the meaning that fits the WHOLE picture the passage paints.
questions.whyHard: Every distractor is tied to a real clue in the text (does not die, does not eat), so the reader must weigh all details together instead of latching onto one.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"The desert fox has huge, wide ears. On a blazing hot day, extra heat leaves the fox's body right through those big ears. This clever trick helps the fox keep its cool while the sun beats down on the sand."

What does **keep its cool** mean in this passage?
choices: Keep its body from getting too hot
Stay calm and not feel scared
Find cold water to drink
Hide in the shade from other animals
questions.correct: Keep its body from getting too hot
questions.hint: This phrase can mean 'stay calm' in everyday talk, but check what the fox's ears are actually doing in the passage.
questions.whyHard: 'Keep your cool' is a well-known idiom for staying calm, which is the tempting trap; context forces the literal temperature meaning instead.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"Most metals are stiff. If you try to bend them too far, they will snap in two. But copper is different. You can bend a copper wire into a circle, twist it around, and straighten it out again, all without breaking it. Copper is very flexible."

What does **flexible** mean here?
choices: Easy to bend without breaking
Hard and stiff like most metals
Shiny and bright in color
Quick to snap into two pieces
questions.correct: Easy to bend without breaking
questions.hint: The word 'but' signals that copper is the OPPOSITE of the other metals. Use that contrast to find the meaning.
questions.whyHard: The reader must track the 'most metals... but copper is different' contrast; one distractor describes the other metals, tempting a child who misses the switch.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"A sponge is full of tiny holes you can barely see. Set it down in a puddle of spilled milk, and the milk seems to vanish. It slips into all those little holes and stays there, so the sponge can absorb the whole spill before it drips off the table."

What does **absorb** mean in this passage?
choices: To soak up and hold inside
To push the liquid away
To make the liquid dry up in the air
To wipe the table until it shines
questions.correct: To soak up and hold inside
questions.hint: Follow where the milk goes in the passage. What happens to it because of the sponge's tiny holes?
questions.whyHard: The correct meaning is never stated; it must be inferred from the chain of clues (holes, milk vanishes, stays there), and each distractor sounds like a believable thing a sponge could do.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Every school should have a garden. Growing vegetables teaches kids patience and shows them where food really comes from. A plain, boring yard can become a place of wonder." Jordan reads this and thinks a garden sounds like too much dirty, sweaty work. What does this show?
choices: The author and Jordan both dislike school gardens
The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work
Jordan agrees that gardens teach kids patience
The author thinks gardens are too much work
questions.correct: The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work
questions.hint: Figure out how the AUTHOR feels first, then how Jordan feels. Do their feelings match?
questions.whyHard: The author's positive view is never stated as 'I like'—it must be inferred from the praise—then held apart from the reader's opposite reaction.

questions.prompt: Read this part of an article about bats: "Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly. They use sounds we cannot hear to find insects at night. One little brown bat can eat thousands of bugs in a single hour. Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world." Which sentence shows the AUTHOR'S opinion, not a fact?
choices: Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly.
They use sounds we cannot hear to find insects at night.
One little brown bat can eat thousands of bugs in a single hour.
Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.
questions.correct: Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.
questions.hint: A fact can be checked and proven. An opinion is what the writer believes or feels. Which sentence could someone disagree with?
questions.whyHard: Every sentence sounds enthusiastic, so the child must separate true facts from the one belief-statement rather than just picking the 'excited' line.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Zoos are wonderful places. They let families see amazing animals up close, and they help protect animals that are in danger. Every city should build one!" Priya reads this. She agrees that zoos help protect animals, but it makes her sad to see animals living in cages. How is Priya's point of view DIFFERENT from the author's?
choices: Priya disagrees that zoos help protect animals
Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places
Priya thinks every city should build a zoo
Priya and the author feel exactly the same way about zoos
questions.correct: Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places
questions.hint: Priya agrees with ONE thing the author said and disagrees with another. Find the part where they don't match.
questions.whyHard: Reader and author partly agree, so the child must hold two ideas at once and locate only the point where the views split.

questions.prompt: Two kids wrote about the SAME field trip to the farm. Sam wrote: "The barn smelled terrible, my shoes got covered in mud, and it rained the whole time." Dana wrote: "We fed baby goats, picked fresh red apples, and learned how cows are milked." Even though they went on the same trip, what can you tell?
choices: Sam and Dana felt the same way about the trip
The field trip was mostly a bad experience
The two writers had different feelings about the trip
Dana must have gone on a different trip than Sam
questions.correct: The two writers had different feelings about the trip
questions.hint: Look at WHICH details each writer chose to share. What do their choices tell you about how each one felt?
questions.whyHard: Neither writer states a feeling; the child must infer two opposing points of view purely from the details each chose to include.

questions.prompt: An author writes that video games are bad for kids because they waste time that could be spent reading books. Leo loves video games. He thinks they help him solve tricky puzzles and make friends online. If Leo wrote a note back to the author, which sentence would BEST show LEO'S own point of view?
choices: You are right that games just waste kids' time.
Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.
Kids should read more books instead of playing games.
Video games are bad for children.
questions.correct: Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.
questions.hint: Three of these sentences match what the AUTHOR believes. Pick the one that shows what LEO believes.
questions.whyHard: Every distractor voices the author's opinion, so the child must resist the familiar author view and select the sentence that carries the reader's opposing view.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Honeybees are helpful. When they land on a flower to drink its sweet nectar, yellow dust called pollen sticks to their fuzzy legs. They carry it to the next flower, and that helps new plants grow."

Text B: "Some farmers say honeybees do not mean to help. Bees only visit flowers to feed themselves. But while they eat, pollen rubs off onto other flowers by accident, so the crops grow better whether the bees mean to help or not."

Both texts agree that bees move pollen between flowers. What do they DISAGREE about?
choices: Whether bees actually touch pollen at all
Whether the bees move pollen on purpose or by accident
Whether pollen helps new plants grow
Whether bees drink nectar from flowers
questions.correct: Whether the bees move pollen on purpose or by accident
questions.hint: Both texts say the SAME thing happens (pollen moves). Look for the one point where the two authors would answer a question differently.
questions.whyHard: Both agree on the fact; the child must infer the subtle difference in WHY it happens (helpful intent vs. accident), which neither text labels as a disagreement.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "The Sahara Desert is one of the hottest places on Earth. In the daytime the sand can get hot enough to burn your feet."

Text B: "People think deserts are always warm, but that is not true. A desert is any place that gets almost no rain. At night the Sahara can get cold enough to freeze water."

How would the AUTHOR of Text B most likely respond to Text A?
choices: "You are wrong, the Sahara is never hot."
"That is true in the daytime, but the Sahara is not hot all the time."
"Deserts are hot because they get almost no rain."
"I agree, deserts are always warm places."
questions.correct: "That is true in the daytime, but the Sahara is not hot all the time."
questions.hint: Text A only talks about daytime. Text B adds information about a different time. Which response uses BOTH facts without calling the other author wrong?
questions.whyHard: Requires synthesizing both texts to see they don't contradict but describe different times; distractors are each half-right (too strong, off-topic, or false).

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Dogs were the first animals people tamed. Long ago, wild dogs came near camps to eat scraps. Over time, the friendliest ones stayed close to people."

Text B: "Cats mostly tamed themselves. When people first stored grain, mice came to eat it, and wild cats came to hunt the mice. People let the cats stay because they kept the mice away."

Based on BOTH texts, what is one thing that was the same about how dogs and cats first came to live with people?
choices: Both animals were caught by people and kept in cages
Both animals first came close because there was food near people
Both animals came to hunt mice that ate people's grain
