Both animals were tamed because they were the friendliest ones
questions.correct: Both animals first came close because there was food near people
questions.hint: Each text gives a different reason food was there, but ask what BOTH stories share. Ignore the details that appear in only one text.
questions.whyHard: The shared cause (food drew them near) is never stated as a similarity; three distractors are true of only ONE text but sound general.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Volcanoes can be scary, but they also help. When a volcano erupts, ash spreads over the land. After the ash breaks down, it makes the soil rich, so plants grow well near old volcanoes."

Text B: "A volcano's eruption can be very dangerous. Hot ash and rock shoot into the air, and thick ash can bury nearby farms and towns."

Both texts mention volcanic ash. Why do the two authors describe the SAME ash so differently?
choices: Text A is about a different kind of ash than Text B
Text A is describing what ash does long after, while Text B describes what it does right away
Text A thinks volcanoes are safe and Text B thinks they are made up
Only Text B is really talking about volcanoes
questions.correct: Text A is describing what ash does long after, while Text B describes what it does right away
questions.hint: The ash is the same. Look for a difference in WHEN each author is talking about — one uses the word 'after.'
questions.whyHard: The child must notice both texts discuss the identical thing (ash) and infer the difference is TIMING (long-term benefit vs. immediate danger), a distinction only implied by 'after' and 'right away'.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Recess is good for kids. Running and playing gives their bodies exercise, which keeps them strong and healthy."

Text B: "Recess helps kids in school. After a break to play outside, kids can pay attention better and learn more when they go back to class."

A teacher wants to argue that recess should NOT be cut to make more class time. Which text gives her the BEST reason, and why?
choices: Text A, because being strong and healthy is more important than school
Text B, because it shows recess actually helps kids do better at learning
Text A, because it proves kids cannot learn without exercise
Text B, because it says kids do not need class time at all
questions.correct: Text B, because it shows recess actually helps kids do better at learning
questions.hint: The teacher's goal is about learning and class time. Which text connects recess to school directly? Then check that the 'why' part is really what that text says.
questions.whyHard: Two-step: first match each text's main point to the teacher's specific goal, then reject options whose reasoning overstates what the text actually claims.

questions.prompt:
Some plants eat bugs! The Venus flytrap has leaves that can snap shut. When a fly lands inside, the leaf closes very fast.

Why does the leaf close FAST?
choices: To trap the fly before it flies away
To keep the hot sun off the plant
To help the plant go to sleep
To make the plant grow taller
questions.correct: To trap the fly before it flies away
questions.hint: Think about what the plant is trying to catch, and what a fly would do if it had time.
questions.whyHard: The reason 'before it flies away' is never stated; the child must infer the plant's goal from why speed matters.

questions.prompt:
First, a seed is planted in the dirt. Then rain and sun help it grow. Soon a small green plant pokes out of the ground.

What does the seed need to GROW?
choices: Rain and sun
Only rain
Only sun
Snow and wind
questions.correct: Rain and sun
questions.hint: Read the middle sentence again and count how many things help the seed.
questions.whyHard: Two distractors are each half-true, so the child must combine both details instead of grabbing the first match.

questions.prompt:
Bees and ants are both insects. A bee can fly to reach flowers. An ant cannot fly. It walks on the ground.

How is a bee DIFFERENT from an ant?
choices: A bee can fly
A bee is an insect
A bee walks on the ground
A bee is very small
questions.correct: A bee can fly
questions.hint: A difference is something that is true for ONE of them but not the other.
questions.whyHard: 'A bee is an insect' is true but is a similarity, not a difference; the child must sort same-vs-different, not just pick a true fact.

questions.prompt:
An owl can turn its head almost all the way around. It has big eyes that see well in the dark. Owls hunt for mice at night.

Why are big eyes helpful to an owl?
choices: They help it see at night
They help it turn its head
They help it fly fast
They help it grow feathers
questions.correct: They help it see at night
questions.hint: Owls hunt when the sky is dark. Ask yourself what big eyes are good for in the dark.
questions.whyHard: Requires linking two sentences (eyes see in the dark + owls hunt at night), and a rival owl trait — turning its head — is a tempting distractor.

questions.prompt:
A bat sleeps all day long. It wakes up when the sun goes down. Then the bat flies out to look for food.

When is a bat awake?
choices: When it is dark outside
Early in the morning
In the middle of the day
When the sun is bright
questions.correct: When it is dark outside
questions.hint: The sun goes down at night. Is it light or dark then?
questions.whyHard: The word 'dark' never appears; the child must infer that 'when the sun goes down' means nighttime and connect it to being awake.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "Bees live in a hive. The hive keeps bees safe. A hive has many little rooms. Bees build the hive from wax." What is this page MOSTLY about?
choices: A hive
One little bee
A wax candle
A tall tree
questions.correct: A hive
questions.hint: Which thing is talked about in almost every sentence?
questions.whyHard: The topic must be inferred from the repeated subject across sentences, and 'one little bee' is a tempting near-miss.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "A turtle has a hard shell. When a big dog comes close, the turtle pulls its head inside. It stays there until the dog walks away." Why does the turtle pull its head inside?
choices: To take a nap
To stay safe from the dog
To eat some lunch
To swim faster
questions.correct: To stay safe from the dog
questions.hint: Think about what happens right before the turtle hides, and what happens after.
questions.whyHard: The reason is never stated; the child must infer cause from the dog's arrival and the turtle's action.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "Owls sleep all day long. Owls wake up when it is night. Owls look for food when they are awake." When do owls look for food?
choices: In the morning
In the middle of the day
At night
At lunch time
questions.correct: At night
questions.hint: First find when owls are awake, then use that to find when they look for food.
questions.whyHard: Requires combining two separate sentences (awake at night + hunt when awake) instead of one stated fact.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact page: "A camel can go many days with no water. A fish must stay in water all the time. Both a camel and a fish need food to live." How are a camel and a fish the SAME?
choices: They both need food
They both live in the sea
They both go days with no water
They both must stay in water
questions.correct: They both need food
questions.hint: Most sentences tell how they are different. Look for the one thing they share.
questions.whyHard: The passage foregrounds differences, so the child must pick out the single shared detail against tempting contrast facts.

questions.prompt: Listen to this fact book: "This book shows how a seed grows. First a seed is planted in the dirt. Next it gets water and sun. Then a tiny green plant pokes up." What would this book MOST likely tell you NEXT?
choices: What the plant looked like as a seed
How the little plant grows bigger and makes flowers
How to bake a loaf of bread
Where the rain comes from
questions.correct: How the little plant grows bigger and makes flowers
questions.hint: The book goes in order. Think about what comes after a tiny plant pokes up.
questions.whyHard: The child must track the sequence and predict the next step, while 'what it looked like as a seed' tempts by going backward instead of forward.

questions.prompt:
Frogs start as tiny eggs in the water. Then they become wiggly tadpoles. Later they grow legs. At last they are grown-up frogs.

What is this passage MOSTLY about?
choices: How a frog grows up
Eggs
Water
Legs
questions.correct: How a frog grows up
questions.hint: One choice ties ALL the sentences together. The others each name just one little part.
questions.whyHard: Every wrong choice is a real detail from the text, so the child must pick the big idea that connects them instead of grabbing a word they saw.

questions.prompt:
Ants are very busy. They dig tunnels under the ground. They carry food to their home. They work together all day.

What is the MAIN TOPIC?
choices: What ants do
Tunnels
Food
The ground
questions.correct: What ants do
questions.hint: Ask: does this choice cover the WHOLE passage, or only one sentence?
questions.whyHard: Distractors are all things the passage mentions, forcing the topic-vs-detail distinction rather than word-matching.

questions.prompt:
The little seed was planted in the dirt. Rain fell on it. The warm sun shined. Soon a green plant popped up.

What helped the seed grow into a plant?
choices: Rain and sun
Only the dirt
A cold wind
The green plant
questions.correct: Rain and sun
questions.hint: Look for TWO things that happened right before the plant popped up.
questions.whyHard: Requires combining two separate sentences (rain + sun) and reasoning about cause, not repeating a single stated fact.

questions.prompt:
Turtles carry their homes on their backs. The hard shell keeps them safe. When there is danger, a turtle hides inside its shell.

Which title fits this passage BEST?
choices: The Turtle's Safe Shell
Turtles Are Slow
Baby Turtles
What Turtles Eat
questions.correct: The Turtle's Safe Shell
questions.hint: Pick the title that matches what THIS passage actually talks about, not just any true turtle fact.
questions.whyHard: Wrong titles sound like believable turtle facts but are never in the text; the child must match the title to the passage's actual topic.

questions.prompt:
Owls sleep all day. At night, they wake up. They hunt for food in the dark. Owls can see very well at night.

Why can owls hunt at night?
choices: They can see well in the dark
They sleep all day
They are big
They like the moon
questions.correct: They can see well in the dark
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells you what owls are good at, then connect it to hunting.
questions.whyHard: The tempting wrong answer ('they sleep all day') is a true fact from the text but not the reason; the child must link the seeing detail to the hunting detail.

questions.prompt:
Sam put on his boots. Then he jumped in the puddle. His socks stayed dry.

Why did his socks stay dry?
choices: Because his boots kept the water out
Because the puddle was empty
Because he jumped very high
Because he took off his socks
questions.correct: Because his boots kept the water out
questions.hint: Think about what Sam put on first, and where his socks were.
questions.whyHard: The cause is never stated; the child must connect the boots (put on first) to the dry socks while three surface details tempt them.

questions.prompt:
A seed needs water and sun to grow. Mia's seed got water but no sun. It did not grow.

Why did Mia's seed not grow?
choices: Because it did not get any sun
Because it got too much water
Because it did not get any water
Because seeds never grow
questions.correct: Because it did not get any sun
questions.hint: The seed needs TWO things. Which one was it missing?
questions.whyHard: Must combine the rule (needs water AND sun) with what the seed actually got; two distractors reuse the mentioned word 'water' to mislead.

questions.prompt:
Ben shared his lunch with Kim. The next day, Kim gave Ben half of her cookie.

Why did Kim give Ben a cookie?
choices: Because Ben had shared his lunch with her
Because Ben asked her for a cookie
Because Kim did not like cookies
Because it was Ben's birthday
questions.correct: Because Ben had shared his lunch with her
questions.hint: Think about what Ben did for Kim the day before.
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring a kindness returned across two days; no sentence says 'because,' and each wrong choice is a plausible everyday reason.

questions.prompt:
Owls sleep in the day. Owls hunt at night. That is when mice come out.

Why do owls hunt at night?
choices: Because that is when mice come out
Because owls cannot see in the day
Because owls sleep at night
Because mice hunt owls
questions.correct: Because that is when mice come out
questions.hint: What else happens at night in the story?
questions.whyHard: Must link the hunting time to the mice appearing across two sentences and reject a tempting swap that contradicts 'owls sleep in the day.'

questions.prompt:
The pond was frozen. The sun came out all day. Now the ducks can swim in the pond.

Why can the ducks swim now?
choices: Because the sun melted the ice
Because the pond was frozen
Because it got colder
Because the ducks flew away
questions.correct: Because the sun melted the ice
questions.hint: What did the sun do to the frozen pond all day?
questions.whyHard: Must chain three details (frozen, sun all day, now swim) to infer melting that the text never states, while 'frozen' is the reason they COULDN'T swim.

questions.prompt:
Most bugs are tiny, but the beetle on the leaf was ENORMOUS. It was bigger than my whole hand!

What does ENORMOUS mean?
choices: Very big
A little bit big
Very heavy
Very old
questions.correct: Very big
questions.hint: Look for two clues: the word 'but' after 'tiny,' and how big the beetle was compared to a hand.
questions.whyHard: The child must combine the contrast clue ('but') with the size clue ('bigger than my whole hand') and reject the milder 'a little bit big.'

questions.prompt:
The old dog was WEARY after the long walk. He flopped down on his bed and fell asleep right away.

What does WEARY mean?
choices: Very tired
Very hungry
Very excited
Very angry
questions.correct: Very tired
questions.hint: Think about what the dog DID after the walk, not just how a dog might feel.
questions.whyHard: The meaning is never stated; it must be inferred from the dog's actions (flopped down, fell asleep), and 'excited' is tempting but is ruled out by falling asleep.

questions.prompt:
The garden was BARE in winter. There were no flowers and no green leaves, just brown dirt everywhere.

What does BARE mean?
choices: Empty, with nothing on it
Covered in snow
Full of flowers
Very colorful
questions.correct: Empty, with nothing on it
questions.hint: It is winter, but read what the garden actually had on it before you choose.
questions.whyHard: The winter setting baits the 'covered in snow' choice, but the text says brown dirt with no flowers or leaves, so the child must use the stated details over the assumption.

questions.prompt:
When the mother bird FED her baby, she put a fat worm into its open mouth. Soon the baby was full and stopped chirping.

What does FED mean?
choices: Gave food to
Gave a bath to
Kept warm
Sang a song to
questions.correct: Gave food to
questions.hint: Two clues tell you: what the mother put in the baby's mouth, and why the baby stopped chirping.
questions.whyHard: All choices are real things a mother bird does, so the child must connect the worm plus 'the baby was full' to land on food rather than another plausible act of care.

questions.prompt:
Rosa was NERVOUS before her first swim lesson. Her hands felt shaky and her tummy had butterflies, but once she got in the pool, she felt just fine.

What does NERVOUS mean?
choices: Worried or scared
Sleepy and slow
Angry and mad
Sick with a cold
questions.correct: Worried or scared
questions.hint: The word 'butterflies' here is not real bugs; think about how her body felt BEFORE the lesson.
questions.whyHard: The child must read the body clues (shaky hands, butterflies) as feelings, resist reading 'butterflies' or 'tummy' literally as sickness, and use 'once she got in... she felt fine' to confirm the feeling came from worry.

questions.prompt:
Milo wants to see the name of his book. But the front cover fell off and is gone! So Milo opens the book to the very first page inside. The name of the book is printed there too.

Which PART of the book is Milo reading now?
choices: The front cover
The title page
The back cover
The spine
questions.correct: The title page
questions.hint: The front cover is gone, so cross it out. Where else does the book's name show up? Think about what the FIRST page inside is called.
questions.whyHard: The name appears on both the front cover and the title page; the child must eliminate the missing front cover and land on the inside first page.

questions.prompt:
Pia wants to know the book's name AND see its big picture, and she wants to do it WITHOUT opening the book.

Which ONE part shows her all of that?
choices: The title page
The front cover
The back cover
The first story page
questions.correct: The front cover
questions.hint: Two parts show the name, but one of them makes you open the book first. Pick the part you can see while the book is still closed.
questions.whyHard: The title page also shows the name, but requires opening the book; the child must combine 'name + picture + still closed' to rule it out.

questions.prompt:
Ben is holding a book. He already looked at the front cover. Then he turns just ONE page. Now he sees the name of the book and the name of the person who wrote it.

What is this page called?
choices: The front cover
The back cover
The title page
The last page
questions.correct: The title page
questions.hint: He has ALREADY passed the front cover, so it can't be that. What is the very first page inside a book called?
questions.whyHard: Requires ordering (a page AFTER the front cover) plus knowing the title-and-author page is the title page, not the cover.

questions.prompt:
Nan wants to find out what a book is about. She does NOT look at the front. She flips the whole book over and reads the very last OUTSIDE part.

Which PART is Nan reading?
choices: The front cover
The title page
The back cover
The last inside page
questions.correct: The back cover
questions.hint: She flipped the book OVER to the outside, not to a page inside. Which cover is on the opposite side from the front?
questions.whyHard: Must distinguish the outside back cover from the last inside page, using 'outside' and 'flipped over' as the deciding clues.

questions.prompt:
A book has two covers, one on each side. The FRONT cover has the title and a picture. Deb is touching the cover on the OTHER side, the one that is not the front.

What is Deb touching?
choices: The title page
The front cover
The back cover
The middle page
questions.correct: The back cover
questions.hint: There are only two covers, and one is the front. If it is not the front cover, what is the other cover called?
questions.whyHard: The title page is a tempting distractor because it is 'not the front,' but it is a page, not a cover; the child must reason that the opposite cover is the back cover.

questions.prompt:
A bug book says: "Pictures by Leo. Words by Ana."

In the book, the picture of the ant is missing two of its legs. Who should fix the picture?
choices: Leo
Ana
The child reading the book
The teacher
questions.correct: Leo
questions.hint: Find out whose job is the pictures. The names tell you who did what.
questions.whyHard: Two-step: map "Pictures by" to the illustrator's job, then to the name Leo — resisting Ana, the author.

questions.prompt:
Mrs. Diaz made a book all about rain. It is only words. It has no pictures at all, on any page.

Which helper did this book NOT need?
choices: An illustrator
An author
A reader
A title
questions.correct: An illustrator
questions.hint: Think about what an illustrator makes. Does this book have any of that?
questions.whyHard: Inference from absence: no pictures means the picture-maker was not needed — the child must reason from what is missing.

questions.prompt:
Sam made the funny drawing of a dancing cat. Pat made up the words that tell the cat's story.

Who is the AUTHOR?
choices: Pat
Sam
Both Sam and Pat
The dancing cat
questions.correct: Pat
questions.hint: The author is the one who makes the words, not the pictures. Match the job to the name.
questions.whyHard: Must match "made up the words" to the author role and pick the second-named person, ignoring the drawer named first.

questions.prompt:
You laughed and laughed at the silly words in a book. You want to make a thank-you card for the person who chose those silly words.

Who should you thank?
choices: The author
The illustrator
The person who colored the pictures
The person who drew the cat
questions.correct: The author
questions.hint: Words are one person's job, pictures are another's. Who makes the words?
questions.whyHard: Three of four choices are picture-makers; the child must tie "words" to the author and reject every tempting picture distractor.

questions.prompt:
A book about the sea has WORDS that tell fish facts. It also has a MAP that shows where the fish live. A map is a kind of picture.

Who made the map?
choices: The illustrator
The author
The fish
The person who reads the book
questions.correct: The illustrator
questions.hint: A map is a picture, even though it shows facts. Whose job is pictures?
questions.whyHard: The map "shows facts," which tempts the child to pick the author; they must remember a map is a picture, so it is the illustrator's work.

questions.prompt:
A book says: "Frogs live near ponds. Frogs catch bugs to eat." The PICTURE shows a shiny green frog sitting on a lily pad.

What do you learn from the PICTURE that the words did NOT tell you?
choices: That the frog is green
That frogs eat bugs
That frogs live near ponds
That the animal is a frog
questions.correct: That the frog is green
questions.hint: Read the words first. Cross out anything the words already told you. What is left can only come from the picture.
questions.whyHard: Must separate picture-only information from facts already stated in the text; three choices are true but come from the words.

questions.prompt:
A book says: "A baby bird cannot fly yet. Its mother brings it food."

Which picture goes BEST with the SECOND sentence?
choices: A mother bird putting a worm in the baby's mouth
A baby bird sitting all alone in a nest
A bird flying high above the trees
An empty nest with no birds in it
questions.correct: A mother bird putting a worm in the baby's mouth
questions.hint: Point to just the second sentence and picture it in your mind. Which choice matches only that sentence?
questions.whyHard: Requires matching one specific sentence to an illustration while rejecting a picture that fits the first sentence instead.

questions.prompt:
A book says: "The turtle hides in its shell. Something scared it!" The PICTURE shows a sneaky fox creeping up behind the turtle.

What does the PICTURE tell you that the words do NOT?
choices: What scared the turtle
That the turtle is hiding
That turtles have shells
That turtles move slowly
questions.correct: What scared the turtle
questions.hint: The words say something scared the turtle but never say what. Look at the picture to fill in the missing part.
questions.whyHard: The child must notice the text leaves a gap ('something') and use the picture to supply the cause, while two distractors are facts the words already gave.

questions.prompt:
A book says: "An ant is tiny. A dog is much bigger." The author drew ONE picture with a tiny ant standing right next to a big dog.

Why did the author put the ant and the dog in the SAME picture?
choices: To show how much bigger the dog is than the ant
To show that the ant and dog are friends
To show where the ant and dog live
To show that the ant is scared of the dog
questions.correct: To show how much bigger the dog is than the ant
questions.hint: Think about what the words are about. The picture is drawn to help you SEE that same idea.
questions.whyHard: Asks for the author's purpose in composing the illustration, matching picture design to the text's main idea rather than reading a single fact.

questions.prompt:
A book says: "Bees make honey. Bees live in a hive." Next to the words is a DIAGRAM of a bee with little arrows pointing to its wings, its legs, and its stripes.

What does the DIAGRAM help you do that the words do NOT?
choices: Point to the parts of a bee's body
Learn that bees make honey
Learn that bees live in a hive
Learn that bees can fly
questions.correct: Point to the parts of a bee's body
questions.hint: What is the diagram showing with its arrows? Two choices are things the words already said, so they can't be the diagram's job.
questions.whyHard: Must identify the special job of a labeled diagram (naming parts) and reject two tempting choices that are true but come straight from the text.

questions.prompt:
Wear a hat on sunny days. A hat keeps the hot sun out of your eyes. Wear boots on rainy days. Boots keep your feet dry.

WHY does the author say to wear a hat?
choices: To keep your feet dry.
To keep the sun out of your eyes.
Because it is raining outside.
To keep your feet warm.
questions.correct: To keep the sun out of your eyes.
questions.hint: The story gives a reason for the hat AND a reason for the boots. Find the one that goes with the hat.
questions.whyHard: The tempting wrong answer is a real reason from the text, but it supports the OTHER point (boots), so the child must match reason to the correct point.

questions.prompt:
Dogs need to go on walks. Walks let dogs run and play. A dog that stays inside all day gets sad.

WHY does the author think dogs need walks?
choices: Because dogs like to smell the grass.
So they can run, play, and not feel sad.
Because walks make dogs sleepy.
So they can meet other dogs.
questions.correct: So they can run, play, and not feel sad.
questions.hint: Look at every sentence. Put together what walks let dogs do AND the problem walks help fix.
questions.whyHard: The answer must combine two separate sentences (run/play + not sad), and the 'sad' clue is stated as a problem to solve, not a direct 'because.'

questions.prompt:
You should plant a garden. A garden gives you fresh food to eat. A garden also gives bees flowers to visit.

WHY does the author say you should plant a garden?
choices: Because gardens are made of dirt.
Because flowers smell nice.
It gives you food and gives bees flowers.
Because gardens need lots of water.
questions.correct: It gives you food and gives bees flowers.
questions.hint: Some answers are true about gardens but are NOT the reasons the author gives. Pick the reasons that are actually in the story.
questions.whyHard: Every distractor is a true or believable garden fact, so the child can't eliminate by 'is this true?'—only by which reasons the author actually states.

questions.prompt:
Firefighters are helpers. They rush into fires to save people. They carry pets out to keep them safe.

WHY does the author say firefighters are helpers?
choices: Because they are helpers.
They save people and pets from fires.
Because fires are very hot.
They save only cats from trees.
questions.correct: They save people and pets from fires.
questions.hint: A reason should EXPLAIN the point, not just say it again. Find the sentence that tells what firefighters do.
questions.whyHard: The top distractor circularly restates the point itself (a classic trap), and another twists a real detail ('only cats'), so the child must give the true supporting reason.

questions.prompt:
Brush your teeth every day. Brushing keeps your teeth clean. Drink milk too. Milk makes your teeth strong.

The author says milk 'makes your teeth strong.' This reason tells you WHY you should —
choices: drink milk.
brush your teeth.
keep your teeth clean.
eat every day.
questions.correct: drink milk.
questions.hint: This story tells you to do two things. Ask: which one does 'makes your teeth strong' belong to?
questions.whyHard: Two points each have their own reason in the same short text; the child must attach the given reason to the correct point instead of the first or nearest one.

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Owls sleep when the sun is up."
Book 2 says: "Owls hunt at night. They fly in the dark."

How are the two books the SAME?
choices: Both books show that owls are awake at night, not in the day.
Both books say that owls eat mice.
Both books say that owls sleep at night.
Both books say that owls cannot see well.
questions.correct: Both books show that owls are awake at night, not in the day.
questions.hint: "Sleep when the sun is up" and "hunt at night" — think about WHEN the owl is busy. Do they point to the same time of day?
questions.whyHard: The shared idea is never stated the same way — the child must see that "sleeps when the sun is up" and "hunts at night" both mean awake at night.

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Penguins live where it is very cold. They cannot fly."
Book 2 says: "Penguins live where it is cold. They swim very well."

What is ONE way the books are DIFFERENT?
choices: Book 1 says penguins cannot fly; Book 2 says penguins swim well.
Book 1 says penguins are cold; Book 2 says penguins are warm.
Book 1 says penguins can fly; Book 2 says penguins cannot swim.
Both books say penguins live where it is hot.
questions.correct: Book 1 says penguins cannot fly; Book 2 says penguins swim well.
questions.hint: Both books agree it is cold — that part is the SAME. Look past it and find the one thing each book says that the other does not.
questions.whyHard: Both texts share the "cold" detail, which lures the child toward a same-answer; they must ignore the match and isolate the truly differing facts.

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Bees make honey. We eat the honey."
Book 2 says: "Bees buzz from flower to flower all day."

What do BOTH books help us know about bees?
choices: Both books show that bees are busy doing a job.
Both books say that bees make honey.
Both books say that bees like flowers.
Both books say that bees can sting.
questions.correct: Both books show that bees are busy doing a job.
questions.hint: One book shows bees making honey, the other shows bees flying all day. What is the same about doing those things?
questions.whyHard: Neither text uses the word "busy" or "job"; the child must infer the shared big idea, while two distractors each repeat a fact from only one book.

questions.prompt:
Book 1 says: "Trees give us shade. Birds build nests in trees."
Book 2 says: "Birds live in trees. Trees clean our air."

What do BOTH books tell us about trees?
choices: Both books tell us that birds are in trees.
Both books tell us that trees give shade.
Both books tell us that trees clean the air.
Both books tell us that trees grow apples.
questions.correct: Both books tell us that birds are in trees.
questions.hint: Each book says two things. Find the ONE idea that shows up in both, even if the words are different. The shade and the clean air each show up only once.
questions.whyHard: The matching idea (birds in trees) is worded differently in each text ("build nests" vs "live in"), while each book's unique fact tempts as a false "both."

questions.prompt:
Read the poem:

Rain taps the glass.
My ball is wet.
My bike is wet.
I sit inside and watch the drops.

How does the child in the poem most likely feel?
choices: Glad it is a sunny day
Sad she cannot play outside
Sleepy and ready for bed
Scared of the rain
questions.correct: Sad she cannot play outside
questions.hint: The poem never says the feeling. Look at where the child is and what her ball and bike are like. What does that tell you?
questions.whyHard: The feeling is never named; the child must infer disappointment from 'wet ball/bike' plus being stuck inside.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:

Sam looked up and saw dark gray clouds. He ran back into his house to grab his coat before walking to the park.

Why did Sam go back for his coat?
choices: He thought it might rain
He was cold from playing
He wanted to show his new coat
The park was far away
questions.correct: He thought it might rain
questions.hint: The story does not say why. Connect what Sam saw in the sky to why someone would need a coat.
questions.whyHard: The reason is unstated; the reader must link 'dark gray clouds' to the idea of rain and needing a coat.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:

Mia planted a tiny seed in a pot. Every day she gave it water and set it in the sun. Soon a small green leaf popped up.

What will most likely happen next?
choices: The plant will keep growing
The plant will die today
Mia will stop watering it
A tall tree will appear right now
questions.correct: The plant will keep growing
questions.hint: Think about the pattern in the story. What has Mia been doing, and what just happened to the seed?
questions.whyHard: Requires predicting from a cause-and-effect pattern (daily care leads to growth) that the text only implies.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:

Ben had two cookies. His friend Tim had none, and Tim looked sad. Ben gave one of his cookies to Tim.

Why did Ben give Tim a cookie?
choices: He wanted to make Tim feel better
He did not like cookies
He had too many to eat
Tim asked him for one
questions.correct: He wanted to make Tim feel better
questions.hint: The story does not tell you why. Notice how Tim was feeling, then think about why a friend would share.
questions.whyHard: The motive is never stated; it must be inferred by connecting Tim's sadness to Ben's choice to share.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem:

The sun goes down.
The stars come out.
I yawn and close my book.
Mom turns off the light.

What is the child about to do?
choices: Go to sleep
Eat breakfast
Go outside to play
Start a brand-new book
questions.correct: Go to sleep
questions.hint: No line says the answer. Put several clues together: the sky, the yawn, the closed book, and the light going off.
questions.whyHard: The reader must combine four separate clues across the poem to reach a conclusion the text never states.

questions.prompt:
Ben saw a big dog. He wanted to pet it, but his mom said, "Wait. Ask the owner first." Ben asked the owner. The owner smiled and said yes. The dog licked Ben's hand.

What lesson does this story teach?
choices: Ask before you touch someone's pet
Big dogs are friendly
Moms know a lot about dogs
Petting dogs is fun
questions.correct: Ask before you touch someone's pet
questions.hint: Think about what Ben's mom told him to do, and why it made things go well.
questions.whyHard: The lesson is never stated; each distractor is a true detail (the dog was friendly, mom was right, petting was fun) but only one is the point of the story.

questions.prompt:
Lily planted a seed. Each day she gave it water. Nothing came up. She wanted to dig it up, but she waited. After many days, a small green leaf pushed out of the dirt.

What is the main lesson of this story?
choices: Digging in dirt is messy
Good things can take time
Seeds need lots of water
Leaves are green
questions.correct: Good things can take time
questions.hint: Notice how Lily almost gave up but kept waiting. What did waiting get her?
questions.whyHard: Requires connecting 'she waited' across several days to the final result; the water and green-leaf distractors are stated facts that tempt a surface reader.

questions.prompt:
Sam broke his sister's toy. He was scared to tell her. He thought about hiding it. Instead, he said, "I broke it. I'm sorry." His sister was still sad, but she gave him a hug.

What does this story mostly teach?
choices: Telling the truth is brave
Toys break easily
Sisters are never mad
Hiding things is easy
questions.correct: Telling the truth is brave
questions.hint: Sam had two choices. Which one did he pick, even though it was hard?
questions.whyHard: The child must infer the message from Sam's choice between hiding and confessing; 'sisters are never mad' is a plausible trap since she hugged him anyway.

questions.prompt:
Rosa was the fastest runner. In the race, her friend Kim tripped and fell. Rosa stopped running and helped Kim up. They finished the race last, holding hands. Rosa smiled the whole time.

What is the lesson of this story?
choices: Winning the race is best
Being a good friend matters more than winning
Running is easy for Rosa
Falling down hurts
questions.correct: Being a good friend matters more than winning
questions.hint: Rosa could have won, but she chose something else. Why was she still smiling at the end?
questions.whyHard: The child must weigh two competing ideas (winning vs. helping) and use Rosa's smile as evidence; 'winning is best' is the tempting wrong answer since she was the fastest.

questions.prompt:
Max wanted the last cookie. So did his little brother. Max saw his brother's sad face. Max broke the cookie in two and gave half away. Both boys ate and laughed together.

What lesson does the story teach?
choices: Cookies taste good
Sharing can make everyone happy
Max is bigger than his brother
Little brothers cry a lot
questions.correct: Sharing can make everyone happy
questions.hint: Think about what Max did after he saw his brother's face, and how both boys felt at the end.
questions.whyHard: Must combine Max's action (splitting the cookie) with the outcome (both laughed) to infer the message; distractors are literal details from the story.

questions.prompt:
The wind blew Mia's hat into the pond. Her little brother started to cry. Mia did not try to get her hat back. Instead, she hugged him and made a silly face until he smiled.

What does this MOST show about Mia?
choices: She is brave
She is caring
She is silly
She is fast
questions.correct: She is caring
questions.hint: Ask yourself WHY Mia let her hat float away. What did she choose to do instead?
questions.whyHard: Two of Mia's actions point to different traits (silly face vs. helping her brother); the child must pick the trait that explains her main choice, which is never stated.

questions.prompt:
Sam pulled on his boots and his thick mittens. He stepped outside. His breath made little white clouds in the air. Not one leaf was on the trees.

When does this story most likely happen?
choices: In spring
In summer
In fall
In winter
questions.correct: In winter
questions.hint: Put ALL the clues together: mittens, breath you can see, and bare trees. Which season fits every clue?
questions.whyHard: The season is never named; the child must combine three clues, and 'fall' is a tempting near-miss because trees can be bare then too.

questions.prompt:
Rosa put some seeds in a cup of dirt. She set the cup on the sunny windowsill. Every single day, she gave it a little water. One morning, a tiny green leaf poked out of the dirt.

Why did the leaf most likely grow?
choices: Because Rosa watered it and gave it light
Because Rosa put the seeds in a cup
Because the dirt got wet one time
Because Rosa poked the leaf
questions.correct: Because Rosa watered it and gave it light
questions.hint: Look for TWO things Rosa did every day. One is told to you, and one is hidden in where she put the cup.
questions.whyHard: Requires combining a stated detail (daily water) with an inferred one (sunny windowsill = light); the other choices each use one real word from the text as a trap.

questions.prompt:
Leo could not find his dog, Pip. He looked and looked. His tummy felt tight. Then he heard a bark! Pip ran up wagging his tail, and Leo let out a big, slow breath.

How did Leo feel at the BEGINNING, and then at the END?
choices: Worried, then happy
Happy, then worried
Angry, then sleepy
Bored, then scared
questions.correct: Worried, then happy
questions.hint: The story never says how Leo feels. Use the body clues: a 'tight tummy' at the start and a 'big breath' at the end.
questions.whyHard: The child must track a feeling change across the story and infer both feelings only from body clues, never from feeling words.

questions.prompt:
Three kids raced to the big oak tree. Nia wore a bright red cap. Tom ran the fastest, but he tripped on a root and fell. The kid in the red cap reached out and touched the tree first.

Who won the race?
choices: Nia
Tom
The oak tree
We cannot tell
questions.correct: Nia
questions.hint: First find out WHO wore the red cap. Then read again to see who the red cap belonged to at the finish.
questions.whyHard: The winner is named only by clothing ('red cap') in one sentence, so the child must link that clue back to Nia in an earlier sentence, while 'fastest' Tom is a strong decoy.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The rough bark scratched my hand.
A sweet song filled the air.

Which words help you feel something by TOUCH?
choices: rough, scratched
sweet song
filled the air
the hand
questions.correct: rough, scratched
questions.hint: Touch words tell how something feels on your skin. Which words could you feel with your hand?
questions.whyHard: Two senses (touch and sound) sit side by side, so the child must match words to the exact sense, not just grab any vivid word.

questions.prompt:
The kite dipped down again.
"Not again," sighed Sam, and let go of the string.

How does Sam most likely feel?
choices: Frustrated
Surprised
Excited
Proud
questions.correct: Frustrated
questions.hint: Put the clues together: the word 'sighed,' the words 'Not again,' and letting go. What feeling do they all point to?
questions.whyHard: The answer requires combining three separate clues (a sigh, the words spoken, and giving up) rather than reading one obvious feeling word.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The storm crashed and boomed.
Dark clouds rolled by.
Cold rain fell down.

Which words help you HEAR the storm?
choices: crashed and boomed
dark clouds
cold rain
rolled by
questions.correct: crashed and boomed
questions.hint: Hearing words are sounds. Say each choice out loud and ask which one is a noise your ears would catch.
questions.whyHard: Every choice is a real sense word (sight, touch, motion), so the child can't guess by 'most descriptive' and must pick the one sense that is sound.

questions.prompt:
The old gate creaked open.
"I missed this place!" Lily said, hugging the fence.

Which words best show that Lily is HAPPY to be back?
choices: 'I missed this place!' and hugging the fence
the gate creaked open
creaked
the old gate
questions.correct: 'I missed this place!' and hugging the fence
questions.hint: A feeling is shown by what a person says and does. A sound word alone does not tell you a feeling.
questions.whyHard: The sensory sound word 'creaked' is a tempting decoy; the child must see the feeling comes from Lily's words and her hug, not the vivid noise.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

Nana's kitchen smelled like warm cookies.
The tile felt cold.
A timer beeped.

Which words appeal to your sense of SMELL?
choices: smelled like warm cookies
felt cold
timer beeped
the tile
questions.correct: smelled like warm cookies
questions.hint: Smell words tell what your nose notices. Which line could you smell, not hear or touch?
questions.whyHard: Three different senses (smell, touch, sound) are all present, so the child must sort them and choose only the one that belongs to the nose.

questions.prompt: Here is the start of a book: "Turtle was slow, but he never gave up. He wanted to win the big race more than anything." What kind of book is this most likely to be?
choices: A make-believe story
A book of real turtle facts
A book that teaches how turtles swim
A true book about a real turtle
questions.correct: A make-believe story
questions.hint: Look for clues about the turtle. Does he have feelings and a wish, or does the book just teach real facts?
questions.whyHard: The topic is a real animal (could seem informational), so the child must use the character's feelings and goal as the fiction signal instead of the subject.

questions.prompt: Two books are about rain. One book tells the true way that clouds make rain. The other is about a raindrop named Dot who is afraid to fall. Which book gives you information?
choices: The book about how clouds make rain
The book about Dot the raindrop
Both books, because both are about rain
The book that is more fun to read
questions.correct: The book about how clouds make rain
questions.hint: Both books share the same topic. Ask which one teaches real, true facts and which one tells a made-up tale.
questions.whyHard: Both books share the topic 'rain,' so the child must sort by the book's purpose (teach vs. tell a tale), not by the subject they have in common.

questions.prompt: Lin's book has a girl who shows you how seeds really grow into plants. Everything the book teaches is true. Is this book more like a story or an information book?
choices: An information book, because it teaches true things
A make-believe story, because it has a girl in it
A story, because stories are always fun
A make-believe tale about a magic seed
questions.correct: An information book, because it teaches true things
questions.hint: A book can have a person in it and still teach real facts. Decide by asking: is the book teaching true things, or telling a made-up tale?
questions.whyHard: The presence of a character strongly pulls toward 'story,' but the child must weigh the true-facts purpose over the character clue (narrative nonfiction).

questions.prompt: Maya wants to find out true facts about how spiders spin their webs. Which book should she choose?
choices: A book that explains how spiders really spin webs
A story about a spider who is late for school
A tale about a spider who cannot find her shoes
A funny book about a spider's silly dream
questions.correct: A book that explains how spiders really spin webs
questions.hint: Every choice is about spiders. Match Maya's goal — learning true facts — to the right kind of book.
questions.whyHard: All four choices share the topic 'spiders,' so the child must match reading purpose to book type rather than picking by the subject.

questions.prompt: Which sentence sounds like it is from a make-believe story?
choices: The little seed yawned and asked the sun to wake it up
A seed needs water and sun to grow
Seeds grow into plants
Some seeds are very small
questions.correct: The little seed yawned and asked the sun to wake it up
questions.hint: Three of these tell real facts about seeds. Find the one where the seed does something a real seed could never do.
questions.whyHard: All four sentences are about seeds and sound simple; the child must detect the single make-believe signal (a seed that yawns and talks) hidden among true facts.

questions.prompt:
Mia and her cat sat by the window. "I see a bird!" Mia said. The cat did not move.

Who is telling this story?
choices: Mia is telling it
The cat is telling it
A narrator who is not in the story
The bird is telling it
questions.correct: A narrator who is not in the story
questions.hint: The word "I" only shows up inside the quote marks. Ask who is telling the WHOLE story, not just who says one line out loud.
questions.whyHard: A character says "I" in dialogue, which tempts kids to name her as the teller, but the story itself is told by an outside narrator.

questions.prompt:
I love bedtime. Every night my dad reads to me. Tonight he read, "The frog jumped over the log."

Who is telling this whole story?
choices: The dad
The frog
The kid whose dad reads to them
The book
questions.correct: The kid whose dad reads to them
questions.hint: There is a little story hidden inside the big story. Find who is telling the BIG story that starts with "I love bedtime."
questions.whyHard: A quoted book line is nested inside the story, so the child must find the outer narrator ("I") and not the character from the quoted book.

questions.prompt:
Ben told his sister about his day. "I fell down and I got up. I ran the race and I won!"

In the part inside the quote marks, who is talking?
choices: Ben
Ben's sister
A narrator outside the story
Ben's teacher
questions.correct: Ben
questions.hint: The first sentence is told by a narrator. But the words inside the quote marks are said out loud by one person. Who is that?
questions.whyHard: The teller shifts between the narrator's sentence and the quoted line, so the child must track who speaks at that specific point rather than who tells the whole thing.

questions.prompt:
Grandma got a note in the mail. It said, "I miss you. Please come visit soon. Love, Sam."

Who is telling the part inside the note?
choices: Grandma
Sam
A narrator
The mail carrier
questions.correct: Sam
questions.hint: Someone wrote the note, and they signed their name at the end. Look for who says "Love, ___."
questions.whyHard: A written note sits inside the story; the frame is told by a narrator but the note's "I" is a different teller, identified only by the signature.

questions.prompt:
My name is Rosa. I have a dog named Max. Max cannot talk, but if he could, he would say, "I want a bone!"

Who is telling most of this story?
choices: Max
Rosa
A narrator not in the story
Rosa's mom
questions.correct: Rosa
questions.hint: There are two "I" voices here. One is only pretend. Find who is telling almost all of the story from start to end.
questions.whyHard: Two different characters use "I," and the dog's imagined quote lures kids away from Rosa, who actually narrates the bulk of the text.

questions.prompt: The picture shows Mia in a coat and a warm hat. The trees behind her have no leaves at all. On the ground, kids are jumping into a big pile of red and orange leaves. When does this story most likely happen?
choices: Fall
Winter
Early spring
A snowy day
questions.correct: Fall
questions.hint: Some clues fit more than one season. Find the one clue that fits only ONE season.
questions.whyHard: Coat, hat, and bare trees pull toward winter; the child must weigh conflicting clues and let the falling red-and-orange leaves decide.

questions.prompt: Sam is holding his shiny new red bike and smiling. But his hands are up near his face, and he is peeking through his fingers at the tall, steep hill in front of him. How does Sam most likely feel?
choices: Excited but a little scared
Happy and calm
Proud and brave
Only very scared
questions.correct: Excited but a little scared
questions.hint: Look at BOTH what his mouth is doing and what his hands are doing. They tell different feelings.
questions.whyHard: Two body-language clues point in opposite directions (smile vs. hiding eyes); the answer must combine them instead of picking just one.

questions.prompt: The picture shows Ben sitting on the floor. Blocks are scattered all around him. A tall block tower lies on its side, broken into pieces. Ben's mouth is open wide. What most likely happened just BEFORE this picture?
choices: Ben knocked over his block tower
Ben is just starting to build a tower
Ben put all his blocks away
Ben found the blocks inside a box
questions.correct: Ben knocked over his block tower
questions.hint: The tower is already broken and the blocks are spread out. Think about what had to happen first to make it look this way.
questions.whyHard: Requires ordering events in time — a scattered, broken tower means it already fell, ruling out the tempting 'starting to build' choice.

questions.prompt: At the lunch table, Tom's plate is empty and he is rubbing his belly with a big smile. Right next to him, Kim's plate is still full of food, and she is looking away with her arms crossed. Who was probably hungry?
choices: Tom
Kim
Both Tom and Kim
Neither one was hungry
questions.correct: Tom
questions.hint: Compare the two plates and what each child's body is doing. Who used up their food?
questions.whyHard: The child must contrast two characters' details at once — empty plate plus a rubbed belly signals hunger, while a full plate and crossed arms signal the opposite.

questions.prompt: The picture shows a dog hiding under a table. Its ears are down and its whole body is shaking. Out the window behind the dog, the sky is dark gray and a bright light flashes. What is the dog most likely afraid of?
choices: The storm outside
The table
Another dog
Being hungry
questions.correct: The storm outside
questions.hint: The reason isn't in the room with the dog. Look out the window for the clue.
questions.whyHard: The cause of the fear is never stated; the child must connect the dog's scared body to the gray sky and flash of light seen through the window.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Mia lost her red ball. She looked for it all day. At night she found it under her bed and hugged it.
Story 2: Sam lost his kite. He looked for a little while, then got a new toy and played happily.

How are Mia and Sam DIFFERENT in how they felt about losing their toy?
choices: Mia cared a lot about her ball, but Sam did not mind losing his kite.
Both Mia and Sam were happy to lose their toys.
Mia got a new toy, and Sam found his old one under the bed.
Both Mia and Sam looked all day and never stopped.
questions.correct: Mia cared a lot about her ball, but Sam did not mind losing his kite.
questions.hint: The stories never say 'cared' or 'did not mind.' Look at what each child DID — how long they searched and how they acted — to figure out their feelings.
questions.whyHard: The feelings are not stated; the child must infer 'cared a lot' vs 'did not mind' from behavior, and one distractor swaps the two stories' facts.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Rosa moved to a new town. At lunch she waved to a girl, and they ate together.
Story 2: Ben moved to a new town. At lunch he sat by himself and looked at the floor.

How did Rosa and Ben feel DIFFERENTLY on their first day?
choices: Rosa felt friendly, but Ben felt lonely.
Both Rosa and Ben felt lonely.
Rosa felt scared, but Ben felt brave.
Both Rosa and Ben made a new friend.
questions.correct: Rosa felt friendly, but Ben felt lonely.
questions.hint: Neither story uses a feeling word. Picture what each child does at lunch and decide which feeling matches each action.
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring opposite feelings from actions (waving/eating together vs. sitting alone), with a distractor that reverses the correct tone.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: A little ant carried a crumb up a big hill. It was tired but did not stop.
Story 2: A little mouse carried a nut up a big hill. Halfway up, it dropped the nut and went home.

How were the ant and the mouse DIFFERENT?
choices: The ant kept going, but the mouse gave up.
Both the ant and the mouse gave up.
The ant dropped its food, but the mouse kept going.
Both the ant and the mouse reached the top.
questions.correct: The ant kept going, but the mouse gave up.
questions.hint: Follow each animal all the way to the end of its story. Who finished the climb and who stopped early?
questions.whyHard: 'Kept going' and 'gave up' are never stated; the child must read to the end of both stories and one distractor flips which animal did which.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: It started to rain. Lily jumped in the puddles and laughed.
Story 2: It started to rain. Max ran inside and shut the door.

How did Lily and Max feel about the rain DIFFERENTLY?
choices: Lily was glad it rained, but Max wanted to get away from it.
Both Lily and Max loved the rain.
Lily ran inside, and Max jumped in the puddles.
Both Lily and Max were sad about the rain.
questions.correct: Lily was glad it rained, but Max wanted to get away from it.
questions.hint: The same thing happened to both kids — it rained. Watch what each one chose to do to know how they felt about it.
questions.whyHard: Same event, opposite feelings that must be inferred from choices to stay or flee; a distractor swaps the two children's actions.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Tom wanted a pet. He fed a stray cat every day. Soon the cat followed him home.
Story 2: Kate wanted a pet. She saw a stray cat but never went near it. The cat ran away.

Both Tom and Kate wanted a pet. Why did Tom get a cat but Kate did not?
choices: Tom fed the cat every day, so it learned to trust him and came home.
Kate chased the cat away on purpose.
Tom already had a pet cat waiting at home.
Kate fed the cat too, but it still ran off.
questions.correct: Tom fed the cat every day, so it learned to trust him and came home.
questions.hint: Compare what Tom did with the cat to what Kate did. Connect his action to why the cat chose to follow him.
questions.whyHard: Multi-step causal inference: the child must link 'fed every day' to 'trust' to 'followed home,' while a distractor misreads 'never went near' as chasing the cat away.

questions.prompt: Mia picked out the biggest slice of cake and put it on her own plate. Then she looked at her little brother Sam, who was staring at the empty tray. Mia sighed, cut her slice in two, and slid half onto Sam's plate. "There," she said quietly. What does Mia's sigh most likely tell us?
choices: She would rather have kept the whole big slice for herself
She was tired from cutting the cake
She did not like the taste of the cake
She was angry that Sam took her plate
questions.correct: She would rather have kept the whole big slice for herself
questions.hint: Think about what she wanted first, and what her sigh shows about how she felt while doing the kind thing.
questions.whyHard: Must infer inner reluctance from a single gesture (the sigh) and contrast it with the generous action she still chooses.

questions.prompt: Every morning, old Mr. Diaz set a bowl of seeds on his windowsill, and the sparrows came to eat. One winter he was sick in bed for a week. When he finally opened the window again, only two sparrows came instead of the usual ten. Why did fewer sparrows most likely come back?
choices: Without food for a week, most of the birds found other places to eat
The sparrows did not like Mr. Diaz anymore
Sparrows only eat seeds in the summer
Mr. Diaz put out a different kind of seed
questions.correct: Without food for a week, most of the birds found other places to eat
questions.hint: Connect what stopped happening while he was sick to what the birds would need to do to survive.
questions.whyHard: Requires linking a cause (a week with no food) across sentences to an unstated effect, rather than picking a reason the text never mentions.

questions.prompt: Ben told everyone he wasn't scared of the tall slide. But at the top, he held the railing tight and didn't move. "Go ahead!" called his friend. Ben looked down, then said, "I think I'll let you go first." Which sentence best shows that Ben was really scared, even though he said he wasn't?
choices: He held the railing tight and didn't move
He told everyone he wasn't scared
His friend called, "Go ahead!"
The slide was tall
questions.correct: He held the railing tight and didn't move
questions.hint: Find the action that his body does, not the words he says. Actions can show a feeling words are hiding.
questions.whyHard: The child must separate what a character says from what his actions reveal, choosing evidence that contradicts his own claim.

questions.prompt: Rosa planted a bean seed and checked it every single day. For a week, nothing happened, and she frowned each time. On Monday she almost forgot to look. When she did, a tiny green sprout had pushed through the dirt. Rosa clapped her hands. How did Rosa's feelings change from the beginning to the end of the story?
choices: From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised
From excited to bored
From scared to calm
From angry to sleepy
questions.correct: From impatient and disappointed to happy and surprised
questions.hint: Notice how she felt while she waited, and how she felt the moment something finally changed. Track both feelings.
questions.whyHard: Requires tracking a character's emotion across the whole passage and matching a pair of feelings, not just one moment.

questions.prompt: The two goats stood on a narrow log over the river. Neither would step back to let the other cross. They pushed and pushed until SPLASH — both goats fell into the cold water. What lesson does this story most likely teach?
choices: If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose
Goats are good at swimming
Rivers are dangerous in winter
It is fun to push your friends
questions.correct: If nobody is willing to give way, everyone can lose
questions.hint: Think about WHY both goats ended up wet. What could they have done differently? The lesson is about their choice.
questions.whyHard: Demands inferring an abstract moral from a concrete event, while a tempting distractor states a true-but-shallow fact from the text.

questions.prompt:
A crow was thirsty and found a jug with a little water at the bottom. Her beak could not reach it. She looked around, then dropped one pebble after another into the jug. Slowly the water rose higher and higher, until at last she could drink.

What is the lesson of this story?
choices: Thinking of a clever plan can solve a hard problem
Crows like to collect shiny pebbles
You should always share your water with others
It is bad to be thirsty on a hot day
questions.correct: Thinking of a clever plan can solve a hard problem
questions.hint: Look at HOW the crow finally got her drink, not just that she was thirsty. What did she do to fix her problem?
questions.whyHard: The moral is never stated; the child must infer it from the crow's actions rather than from any 'lesson' sentence, and every distractor pulls from a true surface detail.

questions.prompt:
A little mouse once gnawed a big lion free from a hunter's net. Long before that, the lion had caught the mouse but let him go. The lion had laughed, 'How could someone so small ever help me?' Now the lion was quiet.

Why does the story end with the lion being quiet?
choices: He learned that even small friends can help you
He was tired from being stuck in the net
He was still hungry and wanted to eat
He was angry that the mouse chewed the ropes
questions.correct: He learned that even small friends can help you
questions.hint: Remember what the lion SAID earlier about the mouse. Why would he stop laughing now?
questions.whyHard: Requires connecting the lion's earlier boast to the ending and inferring an unstated change of heart, not just recalling a fact.

questions.prompt:
A dog carried a bone across a bridge. Below, he saw another dog in the water holding a bone that looked even bigger. He opened his mouth to grab that bone too. His own bone fell with a splash and sank. Only then did he see the water was smooth like a mirror.

What is the main lesson of this story?
choices: Wanting more can make you lose what you already have
Dogs should not walk across narrow bridges
Bones taste better than water
It is dangerous to swim in deep rivers
questions.correct: Wanting more can make you lose what you already have
questions.hint: The 'other dog' was really the dog's own reflection. Think about what he gave up trying to get more.
questions.whyHard: The child must first realize the second dog was a reflection, then reason two steps to the moral about greed—multi-step inference with plausible distractors.

questions.prompt:
The wind and the sun argued about who was stronger. They saw a man in a coat. 'Whoever makes him take off his coat wins,' said the sun. The wind blew hard and cold, but the man pulled his coat tighter. Then the sun shone warm and bright, and soon the man took his coat right off.

What does this story teach?
choices: Being gentle can work better than being forceful
The sun is hotter than the wind is cold
You should always wear a coat outside
Arguing with a friend is a waste of time
questions.correct: Being gentle can work better than being forceful
questions.hint: Compare HOW the wind tried and HOW the sun tried. Which way made the man cooperate?
questions.whyHard: The tempting distractor about the sun being 'hotter' matches the plot literally; the child must abstract the behavior (gentle vs. forceful) into a general lesson.

questions.prompt:
Every day a boy cried 'Wolf!' just to watch the villagers run up the hill for nothing. He laughed each time. One day a real wolf came. The boy screamed 'Wolf!' again and again, but this time nobody came, and the sheep ran away.

Why didn't the villagers come the last time?
choices: They no longer believed the boy after his false alarms
They were too tired from running up the hill
They wanted the wolf to eat the sheep
They did not hear the boy shouting
questions.correct: They no longer believed the boy after his false alarms
questions.hint: Think about all the times before when the boy tricked them. How would that change what they thought when he shouted?
questions.whyHard: Demands cause-and-effect reasoning across the whole story—linking the earlier lies to the villagers' later choice—rather than lifting an answer from the last sentence.

questions.prompt:
When the class pet, a hamster named Nibbles, escaped, Maya froze and covered her eyes. Ben grabbed an empty shoebox, laid it on its side, and put a piece of carrot inside. Soon Nibbles wandered in to eat, and Ben gently closed the lid.

How did Ben respond to the problem differently from Maya?
choices: Ben made a plan to catch Nibbles, while Maya was too scared to move
Ben was scared, while Maya made the plan to catch Nibbles
Both Ben and Maya covered their eyes and waited
Ben chased Nibbles around while Maya fed him carrots
questions.correct: Ben made a plan to catch Nibbles, while Maya was too scared to move
questions.hint: Look at what each child DID. One child acted and one child stayed still. Match the right action to the right name.
questions.whyHard: Must compare two characters' responses at once when neither is labeled with a feeling word, and the swapped-name distractor is tempting.

questions.prompt:
Rosa practiced her jump-rope tricks every day for two weeks. On the day of the contest, she tripped on her very first jump. She picked up the rope, wiped her eyes, and got back in line to try again.

What does "wiped her eyes" most likely tell you about Rosa?
choices: She felt like crying but chose to keep trying anyway
She was sleepy and wanted to go take a nap
She was glad that she had tripped on her jump
She had a piece of the rope stuck in her eye
questions.correct: She felt like crying but chose to keep trying anyway
questions.hint: Think about WHY someone would wipe their eyes right after they tripped in front of everyone. Then look at what she does next.
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring an unstated emotion from a physical action and rejecting a tempting literal reading of 'wiped her eyes.'

questions.prompt:
At first, Sam did not want the new boy, Theo, to join his soccer team. Sam thought Theo would make them lose. But late in the game, Theo passed the ball right to Sam so he could kick in the winning goal. Sam ran over and gave Theo a big high five.

How did Sam's feelings about Theo change during the story?
choices: Sam was worried about Theo at first, then was glad he had joined
Sam liked Theo at first, then wished he would leave the team
Sam felt the same about Theo the whole game and never changed
Sam was angry at Theo from the start all the way to the end
questions.correct: Sam was worried about Theo at first, then was glad he had joined
questions.hint: Compare how Sam felt at the BEGINNING with how he acted at the END. Something happened in between that changed his mind.
questions.whyHard: Must track a character's feelings across the whole story and identify what caused the shift, not just one moment.

questions.prompt:
The storm knocked branches all over Grandpa's yard. Instead of turning on cartoons like she usually did, Ellie put on her boots, went outside, and began stacking the branches into a neat pile. When Grandpa looked out the window, he smiled.

Why did Ellie most likely start stacking the branches?
choices: She wanted to help Grandpa clean up his messy yard
She was searching the yard for the cartoons she missed
She wanted to build a fort out of the branches to play in
Someone told her she was not allowed to watch TV today
questions.correct: She wanted to help Grandpa clean up his messy yard
questions.hint: The word 'instead' is a clue. She gave up something she likes to do. What was she trying to do for Grandpa?
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring the character's motive from her actions plus the 'instead of cartoons' contrast, with each wrong choice fitting a different piece of the text.

questions.prompt:
Kwan's little sister knocked over his block tower right before he could put the last block on top. His hands squeezed into fists, and he opened his mouth to yell. Then he saw her happy little face. He let out a big breath and said, "Do you want to build a new one together?"

Which word best describes how Kwan chose to respond in the end?
choices: Patient
Selfish
Frightened
Bored
questions.correct: Patient
questions.hint: Kwan almost got angry, but focus on the CHOICE he made at the very end. What kind of person stays calm and shares?
questions.whyHard: Must combine the near-anger cues with the final calm choice and match the whole arc to one trait word, while 'selfish' and 'frightened' each echo a moment in the middle.

questions.prompt:
The turtle plods. He plods and plods. Slow . . . slow . . . slow he goes.
The rabbit zips! Zip-zip! He's gone before your nose.

Why did the poet write **slow . . . slow . . . slow** with long dots between the words?
choices: To make the reading feel slow, like the turtle moving
To show the turtle is quietly sleeping
To count how many steps the turtle took
To show the turtle is lost and can't find his way
questions.correct: To make the reading feel slow, like the turtle moving
questions.hint: Read those three words out loud with the dots. What does the spacing do to how FAST you say them?
questions.whyHard: Requires reasoning that spacing and repetition control the reader's PACE, not the literal meaning of the word 'slow.'

questions.prompt:
BOOM went the drum. BOOM-BOOM! The whole room shook.
Then . . . tap. tap. tap — soft as a little mouse's foot.

How do the sounds of the words CHANGE from the start of the poem to the end?
choices: From loud and big to soft and small
From happy to sad
From slow to fast but still very loud
From quiet to suddenly loud
questions.correct: From loud and big to soft and small
questions.hint: Compare the words at the very beginning to the words at the very end. Which are big, which are tiny?
questions.whyHard: Must track a shift across the whole poem and combine two clues (BOOM/shook vs. tap/soft as a mouse) instead of reading one line.

questions.prompt:
"Are we there? Are we there? Are we there yet?" asked Sam again and again.
Mom just smiled. "Not yet, not yet, not yet."

The writer repeats **Are we there?** three times to help you feel that Sam is —
choices: feeling impatient and can't wait to arrive
getting very sleepy in the car
starting to feel carsick
scared of the long, dark road
questions.correct: feeling impatient and can't wait to arrive
questions.hint: The words aren't just asking a question. Why would someone say the SAME thing over and over on a trip?
questions.whyHard: Infers an unstated feeling from the rhythm of a repeated phrase; every distractor is a believable car-trip emotion.

questions.prompt:
The old gate did not just open. It CREAKED and GROANED like it was in pain.

Why did the writer say the gate **groaned like it was in pain**?
choices: To make the old gate seem alive and give it a sad, creepy sound
To warn that someone standing nearby is hurt
To show the gate was just painted a bright new color
To explain that the gate is brand new and shiny
questions.correct: To make the old gate seem alive and give it a sad, creepy sound
questions.hint: A gate can't really feel pain. So what does giving it a 'groan' do to the mood of the sentence?
questions.whyHard: Figurative-language inference — 'in pain' isn't literal; the trap distractor takes the phrase to mean a real person is hurt.

questions.prompt:
A song has this beat:

Clap your hands and stomp your **feet**,
Move to the drum and feel the ______!

Which word best fills the blank so the song KEEPS its beat and still makes sense?
choices: beat
music
floor
song
questions.correct: beat
questions.hint: The missing word has to do two jobs: rhyme with 'feet' AND match a drum.
questions.whyHard: Must satisfy rhyme and meaning at once — 'music' and 'song' fit the meaning but break the rhyme, so the child can't rely on just one clue.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"Sam's dog, Biscuit, wriggled out through the open gate. Sam raced down the sidewalk after him. A friendly mail carrier stepped on Biscuit's leash to stop him. Sam hugged his dog and walked back home."
Which sentence starts the story by giving the problem the rest of the story has to solve?
choices: Sam's dog, Biscuit, wriggled out through the open gate.
Sam raced down the sidewalk after him.
A friendly mail carrier stepped on Biscuit's leash to stop him.
Sam hugged his dog and walked back home.
questions.correct: Sam's dog, Biscuit, wriggled out through the open gate.
questions.hint: The beginning sets up the trouble. Which sentence tells you what goes wrong that everyone then reacts to?
questions.whyHard: All four are real story events in order, so the child must name the structural role of 'problem' rather than pick the first or last thing that happens.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"Nina could not find her library book anywhere. She looked under her bed and inside her backpack. Then she saw that her little brother had used it to build a block tower. Nina laughed and tucked the book safely in her bag."
Which sentence ends the story by finally settling the problem from the beginning?
choices: Nina laughed and tucked the book safely in her bag.
Then she saw that her little brother had used it to build a block tower.
She looked under her bed and inside her backpack.
Nina could not find her library book anywhere.
questions.correct: Nina laughed and tucked the book safely in her bag.
questions.hint: Finding WHERE the book is isn't the same as the story being over. Which sentence shows the trouble is truly fixed?
questions.whyHard: The 'discovery' sentence is a tempting false ending; the child must connect the true ending back to the opening problem to see the book is only settled once it's back in the bag.

questions.prompt:
Read the story. It is missing its last sentence:
"Theo wanted to ride the big kids' roller coaster, but he was always too short. All summer he measured himself against the door frame. On his birthday, he stood next to the ride's sign and finally reached the line. ______"
Which sentence would best END this story?
choices: Theo climbed aboard the roller coaster at last, grinning from ear to ear.
Theo had always been afraid of high places.
The roller coaster was painted a bright, shiny red.
Theo went back to measure himself against the door frame again.
questions.correct: Theo climbed aboard the roller coaster at last, grinning from ear to ear.
questions.hint: A good ending finishes the goal the story started with. Don't pick a brand-new problem or a sentence that just adds a detail.
questions.whyHard: The child must supply an ending that concludes the exact goal set up at the start, rejecting a new-problem sentence, an unrelated detail, and a sentence that loops back to the middle.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"One rainy morning, a lost kitten sat shivering on the Ruiz family's porch. Mr. Ruiz wrapped it in a warm towel right away. The kitten purred and fell fast asleep. The family named her Rainy and kept her forever."
What is the MAIN job of the first sentence?
choices: It introduces a character and the problem the story will solve.
It shows the kitten's problem being solved.
It tells the reader how the story ends.
It explains where the kitten got the name Rainy.
questions.correct: It introduces a character and the problem the story will solve.
questions.hint: The opening sentence's job is to set things up. Notice the name clue is really paid off at the END, not started here.
questions.whyHard: The name-origin distractor invites the child to link 'Rainy' to 'rainy morning,' but that payoff belongs to the ending; they must keep the first sentence's structural job separate from the clue it plants.

questions.prompt:
Read the story:
"Grandpa gave Lea a tiny seed and promised it would surprise her. Lea planted it and watered it every single day. Weeks went by with nothing but plain brown dirt. Then one bright morning, a huge sunflower had bloomed."
Why does the sentence "Weeks went by with nothing but plain brown dirt" belong in the MIDDLE of the story instead of at the end?
choices: It builds up the problem but does not solve it yet.
It introduces Lea and Grandpa for the very first time.
It tells the reader how the whole story turns out.
It describes the setting before anything happens.
questions.correct: It builds up the problem but does not solve it yet.
questions.hint: An ending settles things. Ask yourself: after this sentence, is the reader still waiting to see what happens?
questions.whyHard: The child must reason about why an unresolved, tension-building sentence is middle rather than beginning or ending, matching each distractor to a different structural job it does NOT do.

questions.prompt:
It started to rain. Maya grabbed her boots and ran outside to jump in the puddles. Her sister Nina sighed, curled up on the couch, and pulled a blanket over her legs.

How do Maya and Nina feel differently about the rain?
choices: Maya is excited about the rain, but Nina would rather stay cozy inside
Both girls are excited to splash in the puddles
Nina wants to play outside, but Maya wants to nap
Both girls wish the rain would stop soon
questions.correct: Maya is excited about the rain, but Nina would rather stay cozy inside
questions.hint: Neither girl says how she feels. Look at what each one DOES when it rains, and picture how she must feel.
questions.whyHard: Both characters' points of view must be inferred from their actions (grabbing boots vs. curling up under a blanket), not from any stated feeling.

questions.prompt:
The class was choosing a pet. "Let's get a turtle," said Sam. "They are quiet, and I love just watching them." "Yes, a turtle!" said Priya. "We can each take turns feeding it every single day."

Sam and Priya both want a turtle, but how are their points of view different?
choices: Sam wants a quiet pet to watch, but Priya wants a pet to take care of
Both of them want a pet they can feed every day
Sam wants to feed the turtle, but Priya wants to watch it
Neither one really wants a turtle
questions.correct: Sam wants a quiet pet to watch, but Priya wants a pet to take care of
questions.hint: They agree on WHAT to get, but each gives a different reason. Read what each child says they like about the turtle.
questions.whyHard: The obvious answer ('both want a turtle') is a trap; the difference is in each character's reason, which must be read past the agreement.

questions.prompt:
At the playground, Leo climbed to the top of the tall slide and shouted, "This is the best!" Behind him, Theo held the rail tight, looked down, and whispered, "Maybe I'll just walk back down the steps."

How is Theo's point of view about the slide different from Leo's?
choices: Theo feels nervous about the slide, while Leo feels excited
Theo thinks the slide is fun, just like Leo does
Theo thinks the slide is too short and boring
Both boys are too scared to go down the slide
questions.correct: Theo feels nervous about the slide, while Leo feels excited
questions.hint: Nobody says the words 'scared' or 'excited.' Notice how each boy speaks and what his body does at the top.
