questions.whyHard: Theo's nervous point of view is shown only through clues (gripping the rail, whispering, wanting to walk down), which must be contrasted with Leo's shouted excitement.

questions.prompt:
Snow fell all night. In the morning Ben ran to the window and cheered, "Snow means no school today!" Grandpa looked out at the long driveway, then reached for his shovel by the door. "Going to be a lot of work," he said.

Which sentence best shows that Ben and Grandpa see the snow differently?
choices: Ben thinks the snow is fun, but Grandpa thinks it means hard work
Both Ben and Grandpa are happy about the snow
Grandpa is the one who is excited that school is closed
Ben grabs the shovel to help clear the driveway
questions.correct: Ben thinks the snow is fun, but Grandpa thinks it means hard work
questions.hint: Think about why the SAME snow makes one person cheer and the other reach for a shovel.
questions.whyHard: Requires connecting each character's reaction to the same event and inferring Grandpa's unstated view (work) from the shovel detail alone.

questions.prompt:
The friends talked about the field trip. "I hope we go to the zoo," said Rosa. "I really want to see the lions." Kwan shook his head. "The science museum has way cooler stuff." Mia nodded at Kwan and added, "And it won't be so hot inside there."

Whose point of view is MOST different from the others'?
choices: Rosa, because she wants the zoo while Kwan and Mia want the museum
Kwan, because he is the only one who picks the museum
Mia, because she does not agree with anyone
Nobody has a different point of view
questions.correct: Rosa, because she wants the zoo while Kwan and Mia want the museum
questions.hint: Figure out who agrees with whom first. What does Mia's nod tell you about her side?
questions.whyHard: Three viewpoints must be sorted, and Mia's agreement with Kwan is signaled only by 'nodded at Kwan,' so the reader must group them before seeing Rosa stands alone.

questions.prompt: In one telling of a race story, a slow snail races a fast rabbit. The rabbit stops to nibble carrots, and the snail crawls past to win. In another telling, a slow turtle races a fast deer. The deer lies down for a long nap, and the turtle keeps going to win. What is the SAME in BOTH stories?
choices: The fast animal wins the race in the end.
The slow animal wins because it never stops.
The fast animal stops to eat carrots.
The race takes place in a snowy forest.
questions.correct: The slow animal wins because it never stops.
questions.hint: Look at WHY the slow animal wins in each story, then find the reason that is true for both.
questions.whyHard: Neither story states 'never stops'; the child must infer the shared cause from two different surface details (carrots vs. nap).

questions.prompt: A story from a hot desert land tells of a lost boy who is carried home by a friendly camel. A story from a cold, snowy land tells the very same tale, but the boy is carried home by a friendly reindeer. Why do you think each author chose a different animal?
choices: Because both animals are able to fly.
Because a camel runs faster than a reindeer.
Because each animal fits the land where the story is told.
Because the boy in each story asked for that animal.
questions.correct: Because each animal fits the land where the story is told.
questions.hint: Match each animal to its place. Where would you really find a camel? Where would you find a reindeer?
questions.whyHard: The answer is never stated; the child must connect setting (hot desert / cold snow) to the author's animal choice.

questions.prompt: In one version of a lost-shoe story, a girl named Mei runs from a party and drops a red silk slipper, and a prince finds it. In another version, a girl named Ana leaves a dance and drops a glass shoe, and a king's son finds it. What part of the story is the SAME in both?
choices: A girl loses a shoe and a boy finds it.
The girl who loses the shoe is named Mei.
The lost shoe is made of shiny glass.
The girl dances with the king's own son.
questions.correct: A girl loses a shoe and a boy finds it.
questions.hint: Cross out the details that change between the two stories. What is left that stays true in both?
questions.whyHard: Three distractors are each true in exactly ONE version; the child must separate the shared core plot from version-only details.

questions.prompt: One author tells of a big wolf who huffs and blows down houses while the pigs scream and run. Another author tells the same tale, but the wolf is gentle and only knocks to share a pot of warm soup, and the pigs smile and let him in. Even though the wolf is different, how are the two stories ALIKE?
choices: Both stories feel scary from start to end.
Both wolves blow the little houses down.
Both stories are about a wolf coming to the pigs' houses.
Both stories end with the pigs running away.
questions.correct: Both stories are about a wolf coming to the pigs' houses.
questions.hint: The wolf ACTS differently in each. Look past how he acts and ask what BOTH stories are about.
questions.whyHard: The child must hold two opposite characterizations in mind and abstract the shared event, resisting details that fit only one version.

questions.prompt: Long ago, one group of people made up a story that the dark shapes on the moon are a rabbit resting there. Far away, another group made up a story that those same shapes are the face of a kind old man. How are these two moon stories alike?
choices: Both stories say the moon is really a rabbit.
Both stories try to explain the shapes people see on the moon.
Both stories are about the same old man's face.
Both stories say the moon is made of soft cheese.
questions.correct: Both stories try to explain the shapes people see on the moon.
questions.hint: The two groups name different things, but ask WHY each group made up a story about the moon at all.
questions.whyHard: The child must see past the conflicting images (rabbit vs. face) to infer the shared purpose both stories serve.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Maya carried her painting home slowly, holding it flat so the wet paint would not run. Her little brother Sam ran up the sidewalk to meet her, waving a drawing he had made all by himself. "Look what I did!" he shouted, bumping right into her arm. Maya looked down at the long smear now streaking across her sky. She opened her mouth, then closed it. "Wow, Sam," she said, kneeling to look at his drawing. "Tell me about this part right here."

Why does Maya close her mouth before she speaks?
choices: She decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
She cannot think of anything nice to say about Sam's drawing.
She is too tired from carrying the painting to talk.
She is waiting for Sam to say he is sorry first.
questions.correct: She decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
questions.hint: Notice what Maya was about to do (open her mouth right after seeing the smear) versus what she actually chooses to do instead.
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring a suppressed reaction and a kind intention from a single gesture, with distractors that each fit one nearby detail.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

The whole class had voted to name the class turtle "Rocket." Only Priya had wanted "Shelly." When Mr. Lee announced the winning name, Priya clapped along with everyone and helped tape the new name card to the tank. At recess, her friend Jack asked, "Aren't you upset your name lost?" Priya shrugged. "Everybody gets a say. That's how a class works," she said, and went to line up for kickball.

What does Priya's behavior most show about her?
choices: She can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted.
She never really cared what the turtle was named.
She was afraid the other students would laugh at her.
She changed her mind and now likes the name Rocket better.
questions.correct: She can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted.
questions.hint: Remember that Priya DID want a different name, then look at how she acts and what she tells Jack.
questions.whyHard: Must combine an early detail (she wanted Shelly) with later actions to reject the tempting 'didn't care' reading and land on graceful acceptance.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The old dog does not chase the ball,
He watches from the shady wall.
He knew each fence and every gate
Back when the pup was small and straight.
Now the pup runs, quick and new,
The old dog naps the afternoon through.

What is the poem mainly saying about the old dog and the pup?
choices: The pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age.
The old dog is angry that the pup took his favorite ball.
The pup is lost and the old dog is trying to find him.
The old dog and the pup do not like each other at all.
questions.correct: The pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age.
questions.hint: Compare the words used for the pup (quick, new) with what the old dog does now (watches, naps) across the whole poem.
questions.whyHard: Requires synthesizing a contrast built across separate lines of verse rather than any one stated fact, with distractors drawn from single images.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Every morning, Grandpa set out two cups for tea, even though Grandma had passed away last spring. He would pour hot water into both, then sit quietly until one cup went cold. When Ravi came to visit, he noticed the second cup and started to ask about it, but Grandpa was already smiling out the window at the garden Grandma had planted. Ravi decided to pour himself some tea and sit down beside him.

Why does Grandpa most likely set out two cups each morning?
choices: He is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine.
He forgets each day that Grandma has passed away.
He is expecting Ravi to come over for tea every morning.
He does not like to drink his tea until it has cooled off.
questions.correct: He is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine.
questions.hint: Think about what one cup going cold, and Grandpa smiling at Grandma's garden, suggest together about his feelings.
questions.whyHard: Demands emotional inference linking several gentle clues (the empty second cup, cold tea, the garden) while rejecting a plausible 'he forgets' misread.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Before the spelling bee, Omar had told everyone he would win easily. When he misspelled his very first word and had to sit down, he crossed his arms and muttered that the word wasn't fair. But that night, Omar quietly pulled out the word list and began writing each word three times. He taped the hardest ones to his bathroom mirror. He did not tell anyone what he was doing.

How does Omar change from the beginning of the story to the end?
choices: He stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better.
He decides spelling bees are unfair and gives up on them.
He becomes angrier at the judges as the day goes on.
He asks his friends to help him practice the word list.
questions.correct: He stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better.
questions.hint: Track Omar's actions in order: what he said at first, how he reacted after losing, and what he chose to do that night alone.
questions.whyHard: Character-change question requiring the reader to weigh three sequential behaviors and see the shift, with distractors matching his early or middle mood.

questions.prompt:
All summer, Mole dug and stacked tunnels while the other meadow animals laughed. "Why work on such a sunny day?" they teased, and went back to playing. When the cold rains came and flooded the meadow, the animals had nowhere dry to hide. Then Mole opened his warm tunnels and let everyone crowd inside.

What is the central lesson of this story?
choices: Getting ready ahead of time pays off when hard times come
You should always share your home with others
Moles are better at digging than other animals
Rain can flood a meadow very quickly
questions.correct: Getting ready ahead of time pays off when hard times come
questions.hint: Ask what Mole did all summer that the others did not, and why it mattered later. The main lesson explains the whole story, not just the last line.
questions.whyHard: The warm ending about sharing is a tempting secondary theme, but the central message is about preparing ahead — the child must rank themes, not pick the last event.

questions.prompt:
The proud peacock begged the eagle, "Teach me to fly high into the clouds!" The eagle answered, "Your feathers are too heavy for the sky." The peacock spread his glowing tail and cried, "But no bird is as beautiful as I am!" Then the eagle soared up and disappeared into the clouds, while the peacock stayed on the ground, staring at his own reflection in a pond.

What lesson does the story teach?
choices: What you can do matters more than how you look
Looking beautiful is more important than flying
Eagles are unkind to birds that ask for help
Peacocks would rather stay near ponds than fly
questions.correct: What you can do matters more than how you look
questions.hint: Notice which bird actually reached the sky. Be careful — the peacock's own words tell you what HE believes, not what the story is teaching.
questions.whyHard: One distractor is the character's own boast, which is the opposite of the moral; the child must reject the character's viewpoint and judge it by the outcome.

questions.prompt:
A tired traveler asked an old woman, "How far is it to the town?" "Just keep walking," she said. Annoyed, he snapped, "Give me a real answer!" The woman said nothing. She watched him take a few steps down the road, then called out, "Two hours." "Why didn't you say so before?" he demanded. "Because," she said, "first I had to see how fast you walk."

What is the main message of this story?
choices: A good answer sometimes needs information first
Some questions simply cannot be answered
It is rude to demand answers from strangers
Older people always give the wisest advice
questions.correct: A good answer sometimes needs information first
questions.hint: Focus on the woman's very last sentence — it explains WHY she waited before answering. The lesson lives in her reason.
questions.whyHard: The traveler's rudeness is a real surface theme, and 'unanswerable questions' fits the delay — but the woman's final explanation reveals the true point about needing evidence first.

questions.prompt:
Two frogs tumbled into a deep bucket of cream. One frog looked at the slippery walls and said, "This is hopeless," then stopped moving and sank. The other frog kicked and kicked all night, refusing to quit. By morning his kicking had churned the cream into a lump of solid butter, and he hopped right out to safety.

What lesson does this fable teach?
choices: Not giving up can turn a hopeless problem into a solution
When trouble is too big, it is safer to stay still
Some problems really are impossible to escape
Cream will turn into butter if it is stirred enough
questions.correct: Not giving up can turn a hopeless problem into a solution
questions.hint: Compare the two frogs and what happened to each. The lesson comes from the frog who lived, not the one who quit.
questions.whyHard: Distractors echo the losing frog's belief ('stay still,' 'impossible') and a literal story fact (cream becomes butter), so the child must separate the winning character's lesson from both.

questions.prompt:
A little stream begged the great river, "Let me rush ahead and reach the sea today, like you!" The river said, "Slow down. The sea will still be there." But the stream raced on, splashing wildly over the rocks. It spread out thin across the hot, dry sand and soaked away long before it ever reached the sea. The river flowed on, calm and steady, and arrived that same evening.

What is the central message of this story?
choices: Being steady and patient reaches a goal better than rushing
Bigger and stronger things will always win in the end
Streams should never try to flow toward the sea
Water will always soak away into dry sand
questions.correct: Being steady and patient reaches a goal better than rushing
questions.hint: The stream and river wanted the same thing but ended up differently. Ask WHAT the stream did wrong — was it its size, or the way it moved?
questions.whyHard: The big river succeeds and the small stream fails, tempting a 'bigger is stronger' reading, but the real cause is haste versus patience — the child must find the true reason behind the outcome.

questions.prompt:
Marisol wanted to win the science fair. When her partner Cole suggested a volcano, she said it sounded good, but that night she quietly rebuilt the whole project into a solar oven by herself. The next day she told Cole, "I fixed it so we'd win." Cole didn't say anything, but he stopped bringing his notebook to their meetings and started eating lunch with a different group.

Why did Cole most likely change how he acted?
choices: He felt pushed aside when Marisol changed the project without him
He thought the solar oven was too hard to build
He was excited to try a brand-new group of friends
He forgot his notebook at home by accident
questions.correct: He felt pushed aside when Marisol changed the project without him
questions.hint: Think about how Cole would feel after his idea was replaced overnight, then connect that feeling to the two things he stopped doing.
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring an unstated emotion from Marisol's actions and linking it to two later behaviors, not one stated cause.

questions.prompt:
Every recess, Theo lined his toy cars up in the exact same order and got upset if anyone moved them. When the class got a new student, Priya, she asked to play and reached for a car. Theo pulled it back. But the next day, Theo left one red car at the edge of the mat and said to Priya, "This one can be yours."

What does Theo leaving the red car at the edge of the mat show about how he is changing?
choices: He is starting to share, even though it is hard for him
He no longer cares about his cars at all
He is trying to trick Priya into leaving
He wants Priya to line the cars up for him
questions.correct: He is starting to share, even though it is hard for him
questions.hint: Compare what Theo did on the first day with what he did on the second day, and remember how much order matters to him.
questions.whyHard: Must track a character change across two days and weigh it against established behavior; distractors each fit one detail but not the full pattern.

questions.prompt:
Nadia promised her little brother she would take him to the pond to catch tadpoles on Saturday. On Saturday morning, her best friend called and invited her to a pool party. Nadia looked at her brother, who was already holding the net by the door. She told her friend, "Maybe next time," and picked up her boots.

How does Nadia's choice move the story forward?
choices: Nadia keeps her promise, so she and her brother go to the pond
Nadia goes to the pool party and forgets her brother
Nadia stays home because she cannot decide
Nadia gives the net to her friend instead
questions.correct: Nadia keeps her promise, so she and her brother go to the pond
questions.hint: Notice the very last thing Nadia does before deciding, and what she says to her friend.
questions.whyHard: The answer is signaled only by two small final actions ('Maybe next time,' picking up boots); the tempting distractor matches the party setup.

questions.prompt:
Big storms scared Lena. During thunder, she usually hid under her blanket. One night the power went out and her baby cousin started crying in the dark. Lena's hands were shaking, but she grabbed the flashlight, crawled to her cousin, and started singing a silly song until he laughed.

What do Lena's actions during the storm mostly reveal about her?
choices: She can be brave for someone else even when she is afraid
She is no longer scared of storms at all
She only sings when the power goes out
She wanted the flashlight for herself
questions.correct: She can be brave for someone else even when she is afraid
questions.hint: Look at what Lena feels ('hands shaking') at the same time as what she chooses to do, and who she does it for.
questions.whyHard: Must hold two opposing facts at once (still afraid yet acting brave) rather than picking the simpler 'not scared anymore.'

questions.prompt:
Omar always finished the class relay by running as fast as he could. In the big race, the runner ahead of him tripped and fell right in his path. Omar was about to pass, but he slowed down, helped the boy up, and they crossed the finish line last, together. The coach didn't give Omar a medal, but she wrote his name at the top of the 'Good Sport' board.

Why did the coach put Omar's name on the 'Good Sport' board instead of giving him a medal?
choices: Omar chose to help someone instead of winning
Omar ran faster than everyone in the race
Omar was too tired to finish first
Omar asked the coach for the board instead of a medal
questions.correct: Omar chose to help someone instead of winning
questions.hint: Think about what Omar gave up when he slowed down, and why that would matter to a coach more than speed.
questions.whyHard: Requires connecting Omar's action (giving up the win) to the coach's reason for a different reward, reasoning across cause and consequence.

questions.prompt:
Read this poem.

Stanza 1:
The seed lay sleeping in the ground,
Wrapped up warm, it made no sound.

Stanza 2:
But when the spring rain came to stay,
The little seed woke up one day.

Stanza 3:
Now green and tall, it drinks the sun,
The seed's long sleepy rest is done.

How does the second stanza change the story from the first stanza?
choices: It shows the seed staying quiet and asleep in the ground.
It shows the seed starting to wake up and change.
It shows the seed fully grown and drinking the sun.
It shows a farmer planting the seed in the spring.
questions.correct: It shows the seed starting to wake up and change.
questions.hint: Each stanza is one step in the seed's life. Ask what is different in stanza 2 that was not happening yet in stanza 1.
questions.whyHard: Must track how each stanza marks a different stage and pick the one that names the change, while wrong choices correctly describe stanzas 1 and 3.

questions.prompt:
Read this short play.

Scene 1 (In the kitchen): Mom says, "Ana, if you save ten dollars, you may get a puppy."

Scene 2 (At the park, a week later): Ana counts her coins and smiles. "I finally have enough!"

Scene 3 (At the animal shelter): Ana holds a small brown puppy. "You're coming home with me."

What most likely happened between the end of Scene 2 and the start of Scene 3, even though the play does not show it?
choices: Ana lost all of her coins at the park.
Ana traveled from the park to the animal shelter.
Ana decided she did not want a puppy after all.
Ana gave the ten dollars back to her mom.
questions.correct: Ana traveled from the park to the animal shelter.
questions.hint: A new scene usually jumps to a new place. Look at where Scene 2 ends and where Scene 3 begins, and think about what the gap skips over.
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring that a scene break skips unshown time and travel, not just naming the scene, and each distractor contradicts a detail the scenes already show.

questions.prompt:
Read this poem.

Stanza 1:
The cold rain falls, the sky is gray,
and still I wait for you.

Stanza 2:
The white snow drifts and hides the day,
and still I wait for you.

Stanza 3:
The warm sun shines and clears the way,
and still I wait for you.

Why does the poet most likely end every stanza with the same line?
choices: To show that the weather keeps changing quickly.
To show that the speaker keeps waiting no matter what changes.
To warn the reader that the poem is almost over.
To show that the speaker loves rain, snow, and sun.
questions.correct: To show that the speaker keeps waiting no matter what changes.
questions.hint: The weather is different in each stanza, but one line stays the same. Ask what that unchanging line tells you about the speaker.
questions.whyHard: Must connect a repeated line ACROSS three stanzas to its meaning, contrasting what changes (weather) with what stays the same (the waiting).

questions.prompt:
A chapter book has these chapter titles, in order:

Chapter 1: The Storm
Chapter 2: Alone on the Island
Chapter 3: A Sail on the Horizon

Using the order of the chapter titles, what most likely happens across the whole story?
choices: A boy sails on calm seas and never runs into any trouble.
A boy is caught in a storm, ends up stranded, and may finally be rescued.
A boy builds a boat, sells it, and then buys his own island.
A boy watches a storm from inside his safe, dry house.
questions.correct: A boy is caught in a storm, ends up stranded, and may finally be rescued.
questions.hint: Read the three titles in order like the beginning, middle, and end of one story. What does each title add to the one before it?
questions.whyHard: Must combine three chapter titles into a single plot arc; distractors each fit one title alone but break the order or add events the titles do not support.

questions.prompt:
Read this poem.

Stanza 1:
A lonely lighthouse stands up tall,
above the waves it watches all.

Stanza 2:
The ships slip by in cloudy fog,
past rocky shore and floating log.

Stanza 3:
One ship draws near the jagged stone,
lost in the dark, afraid, alone.

Stanza 4:
The bright light turns and shows the way,
and guides the ship to safe harbor and day.

In which stanza does the poem show the lighthouse doing its most important job?
choices: The first stanza
The second stanza
The third stanza
The fourth stanza
questions.correct: The fourth stanza
questions.hint: Think about what a lighthouse is really FOR. Find the stanza where it actually does that job, not just where it is described or where a ship is in danger.
questions.whyHard: Must judge the FUNCTION of each stanza and decide which shows the lighthouse's true purpose; stanza 3's danger is a tempting near-miss, but the job is done in stanza 4.

questions.prompt: In "The Big Race," Mira practiced alone every day and told her teammates she didn't need their help. On race day she tripped, and with no one nearby, she finished last. In "The Science Fair," Mira built her project by herself and wouldn't let her friends add any ideas. Her project fell apart on stage. Both stories are by the same author. What lesson do they BOTH teach?
choices: Winning is the most important thing
You should work hard to reach your goals
Trying to do everything alone can lead to trouble
Science and sports both take a lot of practice
questions.correct: Trying to do everything alone can lead to trouble
questions.hint: The lesson isn't spelled out. Look at what goes wrong in BOTH stories, then ask what caused it each time.
questions.whyHard: The shared theme is never stated; the child must infer it from two plots and reject the tempting 'work hard' choice, which both stories also show.

questions.prompt: Author Lin writes stories about a dog named Scout. In the first story, Scout lives on a quiet farm and spends his days napping in the sun and slowly herding sheep. In the second story, Scout moves to a busy city, where he spends his days barking at cars and dashing between crowds. By changing where Scout lives, what does the author most likely want readers to notice?
choices: Scout likes the city better than the farm
A new setting can change how a character acts
Scout is a very lazy dog
Dogs should not be allowed to live in cities
questions.correct: A new setting can change how a character acts
questions.hint: Compare what Scout DOES in each place. Then think about why the author would move him at all.
questions.whyHard: Requires linking the setting change to the behavior change as cause-and-effect, instead of grabbing a single detail from just one story.

questions.prompt: The same author wrote two tales about a clever fox. In the first, the fox tricks a crow into dropping its cheese, but later a turtle tricks the fox out of a sweet berry. In the second, the fox fools a rabbit into leaving its carrot, but then a mouse fools the fox out of a nut. How are the PLOTS of the two stories alike?
choices: The fox is always the one who gets tricked
The fox tricks someone, and then gets tricked back
The fox learns to share its food with others
The fox becomes friends with the other animals
questions.correct: The fox tricks someone, and then gets tricked back
questions.hint: Follow the order of events in each story from beginning to end. Both stories have TWO tricks, not one.
questions.whyHard: The child must track a two-part plot pattern across both stories and reject the half-true choice that only describes the second half.

questions.prompt: Kai the inventor stars in two stories by one author. In the first, Kai's flying machine crashes three times before it finally lifts into the air. In the second, Kai's robot breaks down again and again until, on the tenth try, it finally walks. The two machines are very different, yet both stories share the same big idea. What is it?
choices: Building machines is hard work
Kai is not very good at inventing things
If you keep trying, you can succeed
Flying is more exciting than walking
questions.correct: If you keep trying, you can succeed
questions.hint: Ignore what the machines are. Look at the pattern of what happens each time Kai fails, and what happens in the end.
questions.whyHard: The plots differ on the surface (flying machine vs. robot), so the child must see past the details to the shared theme underneath.

questions.prompt: An author wrote two stories about a girl named Rosa. In both stories, Rosa finds a lost pet and returns it to a worried neighbor. But in the first story she searches through a cold, snowy mountain town, and in the second she searches through a hot, dusty desert village. Which sentence BEST compares the two stories?
choices: Rosa faces a completely different problem in each story
Rosa does the same kind deed, but in a different place
The two stories have nothing in common at all
Rosa is kind in one story and unkind in the other
questions.correct: Rosa does the same kind deed, but in a different place
questions.hint: Decide two things: what stays the SAME in both stories, and what CHANGES. The best answer names both.
questions.whyHard: Demands holding a similarity (plot and theme) and a difference (setting) at once, rejecting both the 'all different' and 'nothing in common' traps.

questions.prompt: Our class read a story about a dog named Pip. In the story, Pip lost his red ball. Pip looked and looked, but he could not find it. His tail hung down low. How does Pip feel in the story?
choices: Sad
Happy
Silly
Sleepy
questions.correct: Sad
questions.hint: The words do not say how Pip feels. Look for clues, like what happened to his ball and what his tail is doing.
questions.whyHard: Feeling is never named; the child must infer 'sad' by combining the lost ball with the clue 'his tail hung down low,' and 'happy' is a tempting dog answer.

questions.prompt: The teacher read us a story. In it, the sky filled with dark gray clouds. Right away, the children in the story ran inside to grab their coats. Why did the children run to grab their coats?
choices: It was going to rain
They wanted to play a game
It was time for lunch
They felt too hot
questions.correct: It was going to rain
questions.hint: Think about what dark gray clouds usually mean, then think about why you would want a coat.
questions.whyHard: Requires a two-step inference (dark clouds mean rain, coats keep you dry) with no cause stated; distractors are all normal reasons kids go inside.

questions.prompt: During story time we read about Mia. Mia planted one tiny seed in the dirt. She gave it water every single day. Now a red flower grows where the seed was. What made the flower grow?
choices: Mia gave it water every day
The flower was already there
Mia picked the flower
A bird dropped it there
questions.correct: Mia gave it water every day
questions.hint: Read all the sentences. Find what Mia did over and over before the flower came.
questions.whyHard: The answer is spread across sentences (seed, then daily water, then flower); the child must link the repeated action to the result instead of the last thing mentioned.

questions.prompt: In our book, Sam gave a big yawn. He put on his soft pajamas. Outside his window the sky was dark, and many stars were out. What time is it in the story?
choices: Nighttime
Morning
Lunchtime
After school
questions.correct: Nighttime
questions.hint: You cannot see a clock. Use three clues together: the pajamas, the dark sky, and the stars.
questions.whyHard: Time is never stated; the child must combine three clues, and 'yawn' is a strong lure toward 'morning' unless all clues are weighed together.

questions.prompt: Our class read about a boy named Ben. A new kid sat down with no snack and looked sad. Ben walked over and gave the new kid half of his own snack. Then they both smiled. Why did Ben share his snack?
choices: The new kid had no snack
Ben did not like his snack
Ben wanted more food
The teacher told him to
questions.correct: The new kid had no snack
questions.hint: Look at what you learned about the new kid right before Ben shared.
questions.whyHard: Ben's reason is unstated; the child must infer his kind motive from the earlier detail that the new kid had no snack and looked sad, past plausible self-interest distractors.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Mia put a seed in a pot. She gave it water every day. First a little green stem came up. Then two leaves grew. At last a red flower opened. What happened JUST BEFORE the red flower opened?
choices: Two leaves grew.
The green stem came up.
She put the seed in the pot.
She gave it water.
questions.correct: Two leaves grew.
questions.hint: Think about the order. Find the flower opening, then look at the step right before it.
questions.whyHard: Four real events are all in the story; the child must order them and pick the one immediately before the last, not just any true event.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Sam could not find his red hat. He looked under the bed. He looked in the toy box. He looked by the door. Then Sam found the hat on top of his own head! What is this story MOSTLY about?
choices: Sam looking for his lost hat.
Sam cleaning up his toy box.
Sam getting ready for bed.
Sam playing games by the door.
questions.correct: Sam looking for his lost hat.
questions.hint: Think about the whole story from start to end, not just one part of it.
questions.whyHard: Each wrong choice names a real detail (box, bed, door); the child must summarize the whole story instead of grabbing one detail.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Ben and his dog ran to the park. At the park, it started to rain hard. They ran all the way back home. When they got home, Ben and his dog were wet. Why were Ben and his dog wet?
choices: The rain fell on them.
They jumped into a pool.
They took a warm bath.
They ran too fast.
questions.correct: The rain fell on them.
questions.hint: The story does not say the word 'wet' and 'rain' together. Put the two sentences together to figure it out.
questions.whyHard: The cause of being wet is never stated; the child must connect the rain sentence to the wet sentence to infer it, while every distractor is a believable way to get wet.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The bus came to the stop. Tom got on the bus. He sat down by the window. The bus drove all the way to school. What did Tom do RIGHT AFTER he got on the bus?
choices: He sat down by the window.
The bus came to the stop.
He rode all the way to school.
He waved to say goodbye.
questions.correct: He sat down by the window.
questions.hint: Find the part where Tom gets on. What is the very next thing he does?
questions.whyHard: The child must find a middle event in the sequence, not the first or last, and one distractor (came to the stop) happened just before instead of after.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Lily built a tall tower out of blocks. She was very proud of it. Her cat jumped up onto the table. The blocks came crashing down. Then Lily started to cry. Why did Lily cry?
choices: Her block tower fell down.
Her cat ran far away.
She could not find her blocks.
She was too sleepy.
questions.correct: Her block tower fell down.
questions.hint: The story does not say why she cried. Look at what happened right before the crying.
questions.whyHard: The reason for the feeling is not stated; the child must link the cat, the falling blocks, and the crying across sentences to infer the cause.

questions.prompt:
Tom's teacher rang the bell. All the kids put away their crayons and lined up. Tom hung his coat in his cubby.

WHERE is Tom?
choices: At school
At the library
At a store
At the park
questions.correct: At school
questions.hint: The place is never named. Add up ALL the clues: a teacher, other kids, crayons, and a cubby. What one place has all of those?
questions.whyHard: Setting is inferred, not stated, and the library distractor also fits kids and quiet rules, so the child must weigh several clues together.

questions.prompt:
Someone left muddy paw prints on the kitchen floor. Sam does not have paws. Sam's cat, Milo, was asleep and clean. Sam's dog, Rex, had wet, muddy feet.

WHO made the paw prints?
choices: Rex the dog
Milo the cat
Sam
Sam's mom
questions.correct: Rex the dog
questions.hint: Cross out who could NOT have done it. Who has no paws? Who was clean and asleep? Who is left?
questions.whyHard: Requires multi-step elimination across three sentences instead of reading the answer off one line.

questions.prompt:
Lucy woke up. She ate her cereal. Then a big storm blew the roof right off her playhouse! Lucy felt sad. She put on her shoes.

What is the BIG thing that happened in this story?
choices: A storm blew the roof off the playhouse
Lucy ate her cereal
Lucy put on her shoes
Lucy woke up
questions.correct: A storm blew the roof off the playhouse
questions.hint: Every answer really happened. But which one is the exciting, important event, and which are just small everyday things?
questions.whyHard: The child must separate the ONE major event from three true-but-minor actions, not just find a fact that is in the text.

questions.prompt:
"I lost my kite," said the boy, and he began to cry. A girl walked over. "Here, you can fly mine with me," she said with a big smile.

WHO is being kind in this story?
choices: The girl
The boy
Nobody
The kite
questions.correct: The girl
questions.hint: Kind means doing something nice for someone else. Who offered to share? Being sad is not the same as being unkind.
questions.whyHard: Character trait must be inferred from an action and dialogue, and the crying boy is a tempting pick because he is the first person named.

questions.prompt:
At first, Kip played in the sunny park. Then dark clouds rolled in. Rain poured down, so Kip ran under a big tree to stay dry.

HOW did the story change from the start to the end?
choices: It went from sunny to rainy
It went from day to night
It went from the park to school
It stayed sunny the whole time
questions.correct: It went from sunny to rainy
questions.hint: Look at the beginning and then the end. What was the weather like at first, and what was it like later?
questions.whyHard: The child must track a change in the setting across sentences rather than name a single fixed place.

questions.prompt:
The new puppy was TIMID. When friends came over, it did not play. It hid behind the couch until they left.

What does TIMID mean?
choices: Shy and scared
Loud and playful
Big and strong
Hungry and tired
questions.correct: Shy and scared
questions.hint: Look at what the puppy DID when friends came. Ask yourself why it would hide.
questions.whyHard: No synonym is given; the child must infer the meaning from the puppy's hiding behavior, and 'playful' is a tempting puppy answer.

questions.prompt:
The garden was PARCHED. It had not rained for many, many days. The flowers hung down, and the dirt had cracks in it.

What does PARCHED mean?
choices: Very dry
Very wet
Very green
Very cold
questions.correct: Very dry
questions.hint: Put two clues together: no rain for many days AND cracks in the dirt. What happens then?
questions.whyHard: The child must combine two separate clues (no rain + cracked dirt) to infer 'dry,' and 'green' is a plausible garden trap.

questions.prompt:
My brother is usually calm. But today he was FRANTIC. He ran all around the house, pulling up pillows, looking everywhere for his lost shoe.

What does FRANTIC mean?
choices: Worried and in a hurry
Calm and quiet
Slow and sleepy
Sad and crying
questions.correct: Worried and in a hurry
questions.hint: The word 'but' tells you today is different from calm. Notice how fast he is moving.
questions.whyHard: Requires reading the contrast signal 'but' against 'calm' plus the frantic actions, and 'sad and crying' tempts kids who focus on the lost shoe.

questions.prompt:
After being sick in bed all week, Grandpa felt FEEBLE. He could not lift his own cup. He needed Mom to help him stand up.

What does FEEBLE mean?
choices: Weak
Strong
Angry
Loud
questions.correct: Weak
questions.hint: Think about what Grandpa could NOT do by himself. What does needing help tell you?
questions.whyHard: The meaning is shown only through what Grandpa cannot do (lift a cup, stand alone), so the child infers 'weak' rather than reading it, while 'angry' tempts from being sick.

questions.prompt:
When the puppet bonked its head and fell off the stage, the crowd thought it was HILARIOUS. Every single kid was laughing so hard they could barely breathe.

What does HILARIOUS mean?
choices: Very funny
Very scary
Very sad
Very boring
questions.correct: Very funny
questions.hint: Watch how the crowd reacts. What are all the kids doing?
questions.whyHard: The word's meaning must be inferred from the crowd's laughing reaction, and a fall could plausibly seem 'scary' or 'sad' to a young reader who does not use the reaction clue.

questions.prompt:
Read this:

Step 1: Put bread on a plate.
Step 2: Spread the jam.
Step 3: Eat it up!

What kind of TEXT is this?
choices: A storybook
A poem
A recipe that shows how to make something
A letter to a friend
questions.correct: A recipe that shows how to make something
questions.hint: Look at how the words are set up. Do they tell a story, or do they tell you to DO things in order?
questions.whyHard: 'Eat it up!' feels story-like, so the child must classify from the numbered how-to STEPS, not the fun ending.

questions.prompt:
Read this:

Dear Grandma,
I miss you a lot.
Please come visit soon.
Love,
Sam

What kind of TEXT is this?
choices: A poem
A storybook
A letter
A list of facts
questions.correct: A letter
questions.hint: Notice who it is written TO at the top and who signs it at the bottom.
questions.whyHard: No genre label is given; the child must read the 'Dear...' / 'Love, Sam' clues and infer the text type.

questions.prompt:
Read this:

A duck ran fast.
A duck ran last.
The duck felt sad.
Then it was glad.

How can you tell this is a POEM and not just a story?
choices: It tells about a duck
The words rhyme and come in short lines
It has no pictures
It teaches you a real fact
questions.correct: The words rhyme and come in short lines
questions.hint: A story can have a duck too. Look at the SHAPE of the words and the sounds at the end of each line.
questions.whyHard: The text has a character and events (story features), so the child must judge by FORM (rhyme + short lines), not by content.

questions.prompt:
Two kids picked books.
Maya's book showed steps for how to plant a seed.
Ben's book told a make-believe story about a talking frog.

Whose book is the STORYBOOK?
choices: Maya's book, because it has steps
Ben's book, because it tells a make-believe story with a character
Both books are storybooks
Neither book is a storybook
questions.correct: Ben's book, because it tells a make-believe story with a character
questions.hint: A storybook has made-up characters and things that happen. Which book sounds like that?
questions.whyHard: The child must hold two book descriptions in mind and match genre features to the right one instead of a single text.

questions.prompt:
Read this:

The sun is a star.
The sun is very hot.
The sun gives us light.
Plants need the sun to grow.

What kind of TEXT is this?
choices: A storybook
A poem
A fact book that tells true things
A letter
questions.correct: A fact book that tells true things
questions.hint: Ask yourself: is anything happening to a character, or is the text just telling you real things that are true?
questions.whyHard: It is written in plain sentences like a story, so the child must notice there is no character or plot, only true facts, to name it informational.

questions.prompt: Ben and Mia made a book together. Ben thought up the story and wrote all the words. Mia drew the dog and the park. Who is the AUTHOR of the book?
choices: Ben
Mia
Both Ben and Mia
Neither one
questions.correct: Ben
questions.hint: The author is the one who WRITES the words. Find who did the writing, not the drawing.
questions.whyHard: Must match the 'wrote the words' clue to the author role while resisting the 'they made it together, so both' lure.

questions.prompt: Grace made a book all by herself. She wrote the story AND she drew every picture. How many book jobs did Grace do?
choices: Just one job: author
Just one job: illustrator
Two jobs: author and illustrator
No jobs at all
questions.correct: Two jobs: author and illustrator
questions.hint: Writing the words is one job. Drawing the pictures is a different job. Count both.
questions.whyHard: Two-step: the child must recognize one person can hold both roles and label each job correctly, not just pick the most familiar one.

questions.prompt: The front of a book says: 'Words by Tom. Pictures by Anna.' A girl loved the bright pictures of the fish. Who should she thank for the pictures?
choices: Tom
Anna
Both Tom and Anna
The girl reading it
questions.correct: Anna
questions.hint: Match the person to their job. Who made the PICTURES?
questions.whyHard: Inference from cover credits: the child maps 'pictures by' to the illustrator role and picks the right name, ignoring the first-listed author.

questions.prompt: A book has words on every single page, but it has NO pictures at all. Whose job was left out of this book?
choices: The author's
The illustrator's
The reader's
The writer's
questions.correct: The illustrator's
questions.hint: Think about what is MISSING. Which job makes the pictures?
questions.whyHard: Reasoning backward from an absence: no pictures means the picture-maker's job is missing, and 'writer's' is a tempting synonym for the author.

questions.prompt: The words in a story made a boy laugh at a very funny joke. Who thought up the funny words in the story?
choices: The illustrator
The author
The pictures
The boy
questions.correct: The author
questions.hint: The joke was made of WORDS. Who is in charge of the words?
questions.whyHard: Attributing a specific effect (a joke) to the WORDS, then tracing words back to the author instead of the illustrator who drew the scene.

questions.prompt:
The words say, "Sam ran to the door." In the picture, Sam is holding an umbrella, and rain is falling from the sky.

What does the PICTURE tell us that the WORDS do NOT?
choices: Sam ran.
Sam is going through a door.
It is raining outside.
Sam moved fast.
questions.correct: It is raining outside.
questions.hint: Read the words first. Then look for the ONE thing you only learn by looking at the picture.
questions.whyHard: Child must separate the new fact only the picture adds (rain) from facts the words already gave (ran, door, fast).

questions.prompt:
The words say, "The dog's bowl had no food in it." The picture shows the dog looking at the empty bowl and licking its lips.

How does the dog feel?
choices: The dog is hungry.
The dog is full from a big meal.
The dog is sleepy.
The dog wants to play.
questions.correct: The dog is hungry.
questions.hint: Put two clues together: what the words say about the bowl, AND what the dog is doing in the picture.
questions.whyHard: Combines a word clue (no food) with a picture clue (licking lips) while resisting the tempting 'empty bowl means it just ate' trap.

questions.prompt:
The words say, "The children built something tall in the sand."

Which picture goes BEST with this story?
choices: Children building a tall sandcastle at the beach
Children rolling a tall snowman in the snow
A tall building in a big city
Children playing in a pool of water
questions.correct: Children building a tall sandcastle at the beach
questions.hint: The right picture must match ALL of it: children, building, tall, AND sand.
questions.whyHard: Each wrong choice matches one clue (tall, or snow-building, or water) so the child must check every detail, not just one.

questions.prompt:
The words say, "Ben climbed into bed. Mom read him a book." In the picture, you can see the moon and stars out the window.

What does the picture help us know about the story?
choices: It is nighttime.
The sun is shining.
Ben is at school.
Ben is eating breakfast.
questions.correct: It is nighttime.
questions.hint: The words never say what time it is. Look at the picture for a clue about morning or night.
questions.whyHard: The time of day is stated nowhere in the words; the child must infer it only from the moon and stars in the illustration.

questions.prompt:
The words say, "The bug was tiny." The picture shows the bug sitting right next to a big shoe. The bug is much smaller than the shoe.

Why does the picture put the bug next to a big shoe?
choices: To show how tiny the bug is
To show that the shoe is dirty
To show that the bug can walk
To show that someone lost a shoe
questions.correct: To show how tiny the bug is
questions.hint: Ask yourself: why would the picture show a big thing right beside a small thing?
questions.whyHard: Requires inferring the PURPOSE of the comparison object (the shoe as a size reference), not just reading what is shown.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Pip planted a seed and gave it water every day. At last a flower grew.
Story 2: Bo planted a seed but dug it up the next day to look at it. Nothing grew.

How are Pip and Bo DIFFERENT?
choices: Pip waited, but Bo did not.
Pip grew a flower, but Bo grew a tree.
Pip and Bo both grew flowers.
Pip did not plant a seed, but Bo did.
questions.correct: Pip waited, but Bo did not.
questions.hint: Look at what each one did after planting. Who was willing to wait? The story shows it by their actions.
questions.whyHard: The child must infer the trait 'waited/patient' from behavior (watering daily vs digging up) rather than read it stated; the other choices are false swaps.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: When the big dog barked, Mia hid behind the couch.
Story 2: When the big dog barked, Jon ran inside his house.

How are Mia and Jon ALIKE?
choices: They were both scared of the dog.
They both hid behind the couch.
They both liked the big dog.
They both ran into the house.
questions.correct: They were both scared of the dog.
questions.hint: They did two different things. Ask why someone hides or runs. What feeling makes both happen?
questions.whyHard: The shared feeling 'scared' is never stated; the child must infer one emotion from two different actions (hiding vs running) and reject the choices that fit only one character.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: Tess got cold, so she put on her red coat.
Story 2: Ben got cold, so he sat close to the warm fire.

How are Tess and Ben ALIKE?
choices: They both wanted to get warm.
They both put on a coat.
They both sat by the fire.
They were both too hot.
questions.correct: They both wanted to get warm.
questions.hint: They fixed the same problem in two ways. What problem did both of them have?
questions.whyHard: The child must see that two different solutions (coat vs fire) share the same hidden goal; two distractors are true of only one character and one is the opposite.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: When it rained, Lily ran outside, jumped in the puddles, and laughed.
Story 2: When it rained, Max stayed inside and read his book.

How are Lily and Max DIFFERENT?
choices: Lily had fun in the rain, but Max stayed dry inside.
They both had fun in the rain.
Lily read a book, but Max jumped in puddles.
They were both sad about the rain.
questions.correct: Lily had fun in the rain, but Max stayed dry inside.
questions.hint: Put both parts together: what did each one do, and did each one seem happy? The answer must be true for BOTH.
questions.whyHard: Requires combining each character's action with an inferred feeling (Lily 'had fun' from laughing) into one contrast; the swap and the 'both' choices each break on one half.

questions.prompt:
Story 1: A fox saw a grape up high. He could not reach it, so he walked away and said the grape was sour.
Story 2: A crow saw cheese up in a tree. She flew up and took it.

How are the fox and the crow DIFFERENT?
choices: The crow got her food, but the fox did not.
The fox got his food, but the crow did not.
They both got their food.
They both wanted the grape.
questions.correct: The crow got her food, but the fox did not.
questions.hint: Track each animal to the end: did each one get what it wanted? Then compare the two endings.
questions.whyHard: The child must follow two characters' goals and outcomes across separate stories and compare who succeeded; the swapped and 'both' choices are each half-true and tempting.
